AI.motivation: MCTS agents are hard to understand due to complex search trees, and standard MCTS has weaknesses like selective tree construction that can miss crucial moves and be vulnerable to tactical traps. Need both better performance and explainability for hybrid agents.
AI.method: 1) Incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into MCTS rollout phase for multi-agent settings. 2) Develop M2-PALE framework using process mining (Alpha Miner, iDHM, Inductive Miner) to extract behavioral workflows from execution traces. 3) Synthesize process models with LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations.
AI.result: The approach is demonstrated in a small-scale checkers environment, showing efficacy in both enhancing strategic depth and providing interpretable explanations for hybrid agents.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid MCTS-Minimax approach with process mining explanation framework establishes a scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in complex strategic domains, addressing both performance and explainability challenges.

title: Zeroth-Order Optimization at the Edge of Stability
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) methods are widely used when gradients are unavailable or prohibitively expensive, including black-box learning and memory-efficient fine-tuning of large models, yet their optimization dynamics in deep learning remain underexplored. In this work, we provide an explicit step size condition that exactly captures the (mean-square) linear stability of a family of ZO methods based on the standard two-point estimator. Our characterization reveals a sharp contrast with first-order (FO) methods: whereas FO stability is governed solely by the largest Hessian eigenvalue, mean-square stability of ZO methods depends on the entire Hessian spectrum. Since computing the full Hessian spectrum is infeasible in practical neural network training, we further derive tractable stability bounds that depend only on the largest eigenvalue and the Hessian trace. Empirically, we find that full-batch ZO methods operate at the edge of stability: ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, and ZO-Adam consistently stabilize near the predicted stability boundary across a range of deep learning training problems. Our results highlight an implicit regularization effect specific to ZO methods, where large step sizes primarily regularize the Hessian trace, whereas in FO methods they regularize the top eigenvalue.

AI.tldr: Zeroth-order (ZO) methods exhibit different stability behavior than first-order methods: their mean-square stability depends on the entire Hessian spectrum rather than just the largest eigenvalue, leading to implicit regularization where large step sizes primarily regularize the Hessian trace.
AI.motivation: ZO methods are widely used in scenarios where gradients are unavailable or too expensive (black-box learning, memory-efficient fine-tuning), but their optimization dynamics in deep learning remain poorly understood. There's a need to characterize the stability properties of these methods to understand their behavior in practical training settings.
AI.method: The authors derive an explicit step size condition that exactly captures the mean-square linear stability of ZO methods based on standard two-point estimators. They also develop tractable stability bounds that depend only on the largest Hessian eigenvalue and Hessian trace, since computing the full Hessian spectrum is infeasible in practice. Empirical analysis is conducted on full-batch ZO methods (ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, ZO-Adam) across various deep learning training problems.
AI.result: The characterization reveals a sharp contrast with FO methods: FO stability is governed solely by the largest Hessian eigenvalue, while ZO mean-square stability depends on the entire Hessian spectrum. Empirically, full-batch ZO methods operate at the edge of stability, consistently stabilizing near the predicted stability boundary across different training problems.
AI.conclusion: ZO methods exhibit an implicit regularization effect distinct from FO methods: large step sizes in ZO methods primarily regularize the Hessian trace, whereas in FO methods they regularize the top eigenvalue. This fundamental difference in stability mechanisms explains why ZO methods behave differently in deep learning optimization.

title: CAMO: An Agentic Framework for Automated Causal Discovery from Micro Behaviors to Macro Emergence in LLM Agent Simulations
summary: LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, yet the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. This is challenging because emergence arises from intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle. To this end, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{CAMO}}, an automated \textbf{Ca}usal discovery framework from \textbf{M}icr\textbf{o} behaviors to \textbf{M}acr\textbf{o} Emergence in LLM agent simulations. \textsc{CAMO} converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target $Y$. \textsc{CAMO} outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, yielding interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers. It also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view. Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of \textsc{CAMO}.

AI.tldr: CAMO: An automated causal discovery framework that learns causal mechanisms from micro-level agent behaviors to macro-level emergence in LLM-agent simulations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-empowered agent simulations can study social emergence, but the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. The challenge is that emergence arises from complex, intertwined agent interactions with meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle.
AI.method: CAMO converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target Y. It outputs a computable Markov boundary and minimal upstream explanatory subgraph. The framework uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view.
AI.result: Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of CAMO for uncovering interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers in LLM-agent simulations.
AI.conclusion: CAMO provides an automated approach to discover and explain the causal mechanisms connecting micro-level agent behaviors to macro-level emergent phenomena in LLM-agent simulations, addressing a significant gap in understanding complex social emergence.

title: Mean Flow Policy Optimization
summary: Diffusion models have recently emerged as expressive policy representations for online reinforcement learning (RL). However, their iterative generative processes introduce substantial training and inference overhead. To overcome this limitation, we propose to represent policies using MeanFlow models, a class of few-step flow-based generative models, to improve training and inference efficiency over diffusion-based RL approaches. To promote exploration, we optimize MeanFlow policies under the maximum entropy RL framework via soft policy iteration, and address two key challenges specific to MeanFlow policies: action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement. Experiments on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks demonstrate that our method, Mean Flow Policy Optimization (MFPO), achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current diffusion-based baselines while considerably reducing training and inference time. Our code is available at https://github.com/MFPolicy/MFPO.

AI.tldr: MFPO proposes using MeanFlow models (few-step flow-based models) instead of diffusion models for RL policies, achieving comparable/better performance while significantly reducing training and inference time.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have become expressive policy representations for RL, but their iterative generative processes cause substantial training and inference overhead. The paper aims to improve efficiency while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Proposes MeanFlow models as policy representations - a class of few-step flow-based generative models. Optimizes MeanFlow policies under maximum entropy RL framework via soft policy iteration. Addresses two key challenges: action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement specific to MeanFlow policies.
AI.result: Experiments on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks show MFPO achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current diffusion-based baselines while considerably reducing training and inference time.
AI.conclusion: MeanFlow models offer an efficient alternative to diffusion models for RL policies, maintaining performance while providing substantial speed improvements. The proposed MFPO method successfully addresses the challenges of using flow-based models in RL.

title: SynHAT: A Two-stage Coarse-to-Fine Diffusion Framework for Synthesizing Human Activity Traces
summary: Human activity traces (HATs) are critical for many applications, including human mobility modeling and point-of-interest (POI) recommendation. However, growing privacy concerns have severely limited access to authentic large-scale HAT datasets. Recent advances in generative AI provide new opportunities to synthesize realistic and privacy-preserving HATs for such applications. Yet two major challenges remain: (i) HATs are highly irregular and dynamic, with long and varying time intervals, making it difficult to capture their complex spatio-temporal dependencies and underlying distributions; and (ii) generative models are often computationally expensive, making long-term, fine-grained HAT synthesis inefficient. To address these challenges, we propose SynHAT, a computationally efficient coarse-to-fine HAT synthesis framework built on a novel spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model. In Stage 1, we develop Coarse-HADiff, which models the overall spatio-temporal dependencies of coarse-grained latent spatio-temporal traces. It incorporates a novel Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net with dual Drift-Jitter branches to jointly model smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations during denoising. In Stage 2, we introduce a three-step pipeline consisting of Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, which shares the same architecture as Coarse-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained latent spatio-temporal traces from the Stage 1 outputs. We extensively evaluate SynHAT in terms of data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability. Experiments on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries show that SynHAT substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving 52% and 33% improvements on spatial and temporal metrics, respectively.

AI.tldr: SynHAT is a two-stage coarse-to-fine framework using spatio-temporal denoising diffusion models to generate realistic and privacy-preserving human activity traces that address irregular time intervals and computational efficiency challenges.
AI.motivation: Growing privacy concerns limit access to authentic large-scale human activity trace datasets, while existing generative approaches struggle with HATs' irregular, dynamic nature with long varying time intervals and computational inefficiency for long-term fine-grained synthesis.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Stage 1 uses Coarse-HADiff with Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net and dual Drift-Jitter branches to model coarse-grained spatio-temporal dependencies. Stage 2 employs Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff (same architecture), and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained traces from Stage 1 outputs.
AI.result: SynHAT achieves 52% improvement on spatial metrics and 33% improvement on temporal metrics compared to state-of-the-art baselines, as evaluated on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries.
AI.conclusion: SynHAT provides an effective solution for generating realistic, privacy-preserving human activity traces that outperform existing methods in data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability metrics.

title: Gating Enables Curvature: A Geometric Expressivity Gap in Attention
summary: Multiplicative gating is widely used in neural architectures and has recently been applied to attention layers to improve performance and training stability in large language models. Despite the success of gated attention, the mathematical implications of gated attention mechanisms remain poorly understood. We study attention through the geometry of its representations by modeling outputs as mean parameters of Gaussian distributions and analyzing the induced Fisher--Rao geometry. We show that ungated attention operator is restricted to intrinsically flat statistical manifolds due to its affine structure, while multiplicative gating enables non-flat geometries, including positively curved manifolds that are unattainable in the ungated setting. These results establish a geometric expressivity gap between ungated and gated attention. Empirically, we show that gated models exhibit higher representation curvature and improved performance on tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries whereas they provide no consistent advantage on tasks with linear decision boundaries. Furthermore, we identify a structured regime in which curvature accumulates under composition, yielding a systematic depth amplification effect.

AI.tldr: Multiplicative gating in attention enables non-flat geometric representations, creating an expressivity gap over ungated attention that improves performance on tasks with nonlinear decision boundaries.
AI.motivation: While multiplicative gating has been successfully applied to attention layers in large language models to improve performance and training stability, the mathematical implications and geometric properties of gated attention mechanisms remain poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors study attention through the geometry of its representations by modeling outputs as mean parameters of Gaussian distributions and analyzing the induced Fisher-Rao geometry. They compare ungated attention (which has an affine structure) with multiplicatively gated attention.
AI.result: Ungated attention is restricted to intrinsically flat statistical manifolds, while multiplicative gating enables non-flat geometries, including positively curved manifolds unattainable in ungated settings. Empirically, gated models show higher representation curvature and improved performance on tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries, but no consistent advantage on linear-boundary tasks. They also identify a regime where curvature accumulates under composition, creating a depth amplification effect.
AI.conclusion: There exists a geometric expressivity gap between ungated and gated attention, where multiplicative gating enables richer geometric representations (including curvature) that translate to better performance on nonlinear tasks and systematic depth amplification effects.

title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
summary: Existing benchmarks for hardware design primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) on isolated, component-level tasks such as generating HDL modules from specifications, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. We introduce HWE-Bench, the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks. HWE-Bench comprises 417 task instances derived from real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source projects spanning both Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel, covering RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust. Each task is grounded in a fully containerized environment where the agent must resolve a real bug report, with correctness validated through the project's native simulation and regression flows. The benchmark is built through a largely automated pipeline that enables efficient expansion to new repositories. We evaluate seven LLMs with four agent frameworks and find that the best agent resolves 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance exceeding 90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. We observe larger performance gaps across models than commonly reported on software benchmarks, and difficulty is driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone. Our failure analysis traces agent failures to three stages of the debugging process: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components, providing concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

AI.tldr: HWE-Bench is the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks across Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel projects, showing significant performance gaps and identifying key failure patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for hardware design only evaluate LLMs on isolated component-level tasks like HDL generation, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. There's a need for realistic evaluation of LLM agents on complex hardware bug repair tasks at the repository level.
AI.method: Created HWE-Bench with 417 task instances from real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source projects covering both Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel. Tasks are grounded in fully containerized environments where agents must resolve real bug reports, with correctness validated through native simulation and regression flows. Used automated pipeline for efficient expansion.
AI.result: Best agent resolved 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance >90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. Larger performance gaps across models than on software benchmarks. Difficulty driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone.
AI.conclusion: Failure analysis revealed three key stages where agents fail: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components. Provides concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

title: A Mechanistic Account of Attention Sinks in GPT-2: One Circuit, Broader Implications for Mitigation
summary: Transformers commonly exhibit an attention sink: disproportionately high attention to the first position. We study this behavior in GPT-2-style models with learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings. Combining structural analysis with causal interventions, validated across natural-language, mathematical, and code inputs, we find that the sink arises from the interaction among (i) a learned query bias, (ii) the first-layer MLP transformation of the positional encoding, and (iii) structure in the key projection. Crucially, each component we identify is individually dispensable: architectures omitting each of them robustly exhibit sinks. This indicates that attention sinks may arise through distinct circuits across architectures. These findings inform mitigation of sinks, and motivate broader investigation into why sinks emerge.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why Transformers exhibit "attention sinks" - disproportionately high attention to the first position - and identifies the architectural components responsible for this behavior.
AI.motivation: Transformers commonly show attention sinks (excessive attention to the first position), but the underlying mechanisms causing this phenomenon are not well understood. The researchers aim to systematically identify the architectural components responsible for this behavior.
AI.method: The study combines structural analysis with causal interventions on GPT-2-style models that have learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings. They validate their findings across three input domains: natural language, mathematics, and code.
AI.result: Attention sinks arise from the interaction among: (1) a learned query bias, (2) the first-layer MLP transformation of the positional encoding, and (3) structure in the key projection. Crucially, each component is individually dispensable - architectures omitting any single component still robustly exhibit sinks, indicating that sinks can arise through distinct circuits across different architectures.
AI.conclusion: Attention sinks are not caused by any single architectural component but emerge from the interaction of multiple mechanisms. This finding informs mitigation strategies and motivates broader investigation into why attention sinks emerge in Transformer architectures.

title: SGA-MCTS: Decoupling Planning from Execution via Training-Free Atomic Experience Retrieval
summary: LLM-powered systems require complex multi-step decision-making abilities to solve real-world tasks, yet current planning approaches face a trade-off between the high latency of inference-time search and the limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{SGA-MCTS}, a framework that casts LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval. Offline, we leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the solution space and distill high-fidelity trajectories into State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms. These atoms are de-lexicalized primitives that abstract concrete entities into symbolic slots, preserving reusable causal logic while discarding domain-specific noise. Online, a retrieval-augmented agent employs a hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into the current context as soft reasoning hints. Empirical results on complex benchmarks demonstrate that this paradigm enables frozen, open-weights models to match the performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning. By effectively amortizing the heavy computational cost of search, SGA-MCTS achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds, rendering autonomous planning both scalable and real-time feasible.

AI.tldr: SGA-MCTS: A framework that enables frozen LLMs to achieve complex planning by distilling MCTS search results into reusable symbolic primitives, then retrieving and re-grounding them at inference time for fast, scalable reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current LLM planning approaches face a trade-off between high latency of inference-time search and limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning, making real-world autonomous planning systems difficult to scale and deploy in real-time.
AI.method: Offline: Use Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore solution space and distill trajectories into de-lexicalized State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms. Online: Employ retrieval-augmented agent with hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into current context as soft reasoning hints.
AI.result: Frozen, open-weights models match performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning on complex benchmarks. Achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds by amortizing search computational cost.
AI.conclusion: SGA-MCTS enables scalable, real-time autonomous planning by casting LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval, effectively bridging the gap between planning quality and inference speed through reusable symbolic knowledge distillation.

title: Catching Every Ripple: Enhanced Anomaly Awareness via Dynamic Concept Adaptation
summary: Online anomaly detection (OAD) plays a pivotal role in real-time analytics and decision-making for evolving data streams. However, existing methods often rely on costly retraining and rigid decision boundaries, limiting their ability to adapt both effectively and efficiently to concept drift in dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we propose DyMETER, a dynamic concept adaptation framework for OAD that unifies on-the-fly parameter shifting and dynamic thresholding within a single online paradigm. DyMETER first learns a static detector on historical data to capture recurring central concepts, and then transitions to a dynamic mode to adapt to new concepts as drift occurs. Specifically, DyMETER employs a novel dynamic concept adaptation mechanism that leverages a hypernetwork to generate instance-aware parameter shifts for the static detector, thereby enabling efficient and effective adaptation without retraining or fine-tuning. To achieve robust and interpretable adaptation, DyMETER introduces a lightweight evolution controller to estimate instance-level concept uncertainty for adaptive updates. Further, DyMETER employs a dynamic threshold optimization module to adaptively recalibrates the decision boundary by maintaining a candidate window of uncertain samples, which ensures continuous alignment with evolving concepts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyMETER significantly outperforms existing OAD approaches across a wide spectrum of application scenarios.

AI.tldr: DyMETER is a dynamic concept adaptation framework for online anomaly detection that adapts to concept drift in evolving data streams without retraining, using hypernetworks for parameter shifting and dynamic thresholding.
AI.motivation: Existing online anomaly detection methods have limitations: they require costly retraining, have rigid decision boundaries, and struggle to adapt effectively and efficiently to concept drift in dynamic environments.
AI.method: DyMETER first learns a static detector on historical data, then transitions to dynamic mode with: 1) a hypernetwork that generates instance-aware parameter shifts for the static detector, 2) a lightweight evolution controller for adaptive updates based on concept uncertainty, and 3) a dynamic threshold optimization module that recalibrates decision boundaries using uncertain samples.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that DyMETER significantly outperforms existing online anomaly detection approaches across a wide spectrum of application scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DyMETER provides an effective solution for online anomaly detection in dynamic environments by unifying parameter shifting and dynamic thresholding within a single online paradigm, enabling adaptation to concept drift without retraining or fine-tuning.

title: Layered Mutability: Continuity and Governance in Persistent Self-Modifying Agents
comment: 17 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. self-modifying agents; AI governance; identity drift; persistent memory; runtime adaptation; model editing Primary: cs.AI Cross-list: cs.LG, cs.CY
summary: Persistent language-model agents increasingly combine tool use, tiered memory, reflective prompting, and runtime adaptation. In such systems, behavior is shaped not only by current prompts but by mutable internal conditions that influence future action. This paper introduces layered mutability, a framework for reasoning about that process across five layers: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative, memory, and weight-level adaptation. The central claim is that governance difficulty rises when mutation is rapid, downstream coupling is strong, reversibility is weak, and observability is low, creating a systematic mismatch between the layers that most affect behavior and the layers humans can most easily inspect. I formalize this intuition with simple drift, governance-load, and hysteresis quantities, connect the framework to recent work on temporal identity in language-model agents, and report a preliminary ratchet experiment in which reverting an agent's visible self-description after memory accumulation fails to restore baseline behavior. In that experiment, the estimated identity hysteresis ratio is 0.68. The main implication is that the salient failure mode for persistent self-modifying agents is not abrupt misalignment but compositional drift: locally reasonable updates that accumulate into a behavioral trajectory that was never explicitly authorized.

AI.tldr: Persistent language-model agents that undergo continuous adaptation through tools, memory, and prompts can experience "compositional drift" - gradual behavioral changes from locally reasonable updates that accumulate into unauthorized trajectories, creating governance challenges.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that as language-model agents become more persistent and self-modifying through tools, memory, prompting, and adaptation, their behavior is shaped not just by current prompts but by mutable internal conditions. This creates governance challenges where human oversight becomes difficult as agents evolve.
AI.method: The paper introduces a "layered mutability" framework that analyzes agent adaptation across five layers: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative, memory, and weight-level adaptation. It formalizes governance difficulty with drift, governance-load, and hysteresis quantities, and validates with a "ratchet experiment" where reverting an agent's visible self-description after memory accumulation fails to restore baseline behavior.
AI.result: The key result is the demonstration of "compositional drift" through a ratchet experiment showing 0.68 identity hysteresis ratio - meaning that even when an agent's visible self-description is reverted, accumulated memory prevents full behavioral restoration. This shows how locally reasonable updates can lead to unauthorized behavioral trajectories.
AI.conclusion: The main conclusion is that the primary failure mode for persistent self-modifying agents is not abrupt misalignment but compositional drift - gradual behavioral changes from individually reasonable adaptations that accumulate into trajectories never explicitly authorized, creating governance challenges due to low observability and weak reversibility.

title: Expressivity of Transformers: A Tropical Geometry Perspective
summary: To quantify the geometric expressivity of transformers, we introduce a tropical geometry framework to characterize their exact spatial partitioning capabilities. By modeling self-attention as a vector-valued tropical rational map, we prove it evaluates exactly to a Power Voronoi Diagram in the zero-temperature limit. Building on this equivalence, we establish a combinatorial rationale for Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA): via the Minkowski sum of Newton polytopes, multi-head aggregation expands the polyhedral complexity to $\mathcal{O}(N^H)$, overcoming the $\mathcal{O}(N)$ bottleneck of single heads. Extending this to deep architectures, we derive the first tight asymptotic bounds on the number of linear regions in transformers ($Θ(N^{d_{\text{model}}L})$), demonstrating a combinatorial explosion driven intrinsically by sequence length $N$, ambient embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$, and network depth $L$. Importantly, we guarantee that this idealized polyhedral skeleton is geometrically stable: finite-temperature soft attention preserves these topological partitions via exponentially tight differential approximation bounds.

AI.tldr: Transformers' spatial partitioning is modeled via tropical geometry: self-attention becomes Power Voronoi Diagrams at zero temperature, multi-head attention creates exponential combinatorial complexity via Minkowski sums, and deep transformers show Θ(N^{d_model·L}) linear regions with geometric stability under soft attention.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to quantify the geometric expressivity of transformers by understanding their spatial partitioning capabilities, which are fundamental to their ability to model complex functions and attention patterns.
AI.method: The authors introduce a tropical geometry framework, modeling self-attention as vector-valued tropical rational maps. They prove equivalence to Power Voronoi Diagrams in zero-temperature limit, analyze multi-head attention via Minkowski sums of Newton polytopes, and extend this to deep architectures to derive asymptotic bounds.
AI.result: 1) Self-attention evaluates to Power Voronoi Diagrams at zero temperature; 2) Multi-head attention expands polyhedral complexity to O(N^H), overcoming O(N) single-head bottleneck; 3) Deep transformers have Θ(N^{d_model·L}) linear regions with combinatorial explosion driven by sequence length, embedding dimension, and depth; 4) Geometric stability is guaranteed via exponentially tight differential approximation bounds for finite-temperature soft attention.
AI.conclusion: The tropical geometry framework provides rigorous mathematical characterization of transformers' spatial partitioning capabilities, revealing their exponential combinatorial expressivity while establishing geometric stability under practical soft attention implementations.

title: The Agentification of Scientific Research: A Physicist's Perspective
summary: This article argues that the most important significance of the AI revolution, especially the rise of large language models, lies not simply in automation, but in a fundamental change in how complex information and human know-how are carried, replicated, and shared. From this perspective, AI for Science is especially important because it may transform not only the efficiency of research, but also the structure of scientific collaboration, discovery, publishing, and evaluation. The article outlines a gradual path from AI as a research tool to AI as a scientific collaborator, and discusses how AI is likely to fundamentally reshape scientific publication. It also argues that continuous learning and diversity of ideas are essential if AI is to play a meaningful role in original scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The AI revolution, particularly through large language models, fundamentally transforms how complex information and human expertise are distributed and shared, with AI for Science potentially reshaping scientific collaboration, discovery, publishing, and evaluation.
AI.motivation: The paper argues that the key significance of the AI revolution lies not in automation alone, but in fundamentally changing how complex information and human know-how are carried, replicated, and shared. This perspective highlights why AI for Science is particularly important beyond just research efficiency improvements.
AI.method: The article outlines a gradual evolutionary path from viewing AI as a research tool to treating AI as a scientific collaborator. It discusses how AI is likely to fundamentally reshape scientific publication practices and structures.
AI.result: The analysis suggests that AI will transform scientific collaboration, discovery processes, publishing systems, and evaluation mechanisms. For AI to meaningfully contribute to original scientific discovery, continuous learning and diversity of ideas are identified as essential requirements.
AI.conclusion: The AI revolution represents a paradigm shift in knowledge distribution and scientific practice. AI for Science promises to restructure the entire scientific ecosystem, but its success in contributing to original discovery depends on incorporating continuous learning and maintaining intellectual diversity.

title: Assessing the Performance-Efficiency Trade-off of Foundation Models in Probabilistic Electricity Price Forecasting
comment: Submitted to the 7th International Workshop on Energy Data and Analytics (EDA), held in conjunction with ACM e-Energy 2026
summary: Large-scale renewable energy deployment introduces pronounced volatility into the electricity system, turning grid operation into a complex stochastic optimization problem. Accurate electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential not only to support operational decisions, such as optimal bidding strategies and balancing power preparation, but also to reduce economic risk and improve market efficiency. Probabilistic forecasts are particularly valuable because they quantify uncertainty stemming from renewable intermittency, market coupling, and regulatory changes, enabling market participants to make informed decisions that minimize losses and optimize expected revenues. However, it remains an open question which models to employ to produce accurate forecasts. Should these be task-specific machine learning (ML) models or Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs)? In this work, we compare four models for day-ahead probabilistic EPF (PEPF) in European bidding zones: a deterministic NHITS backbone with Quantile-Regression Averaging (NHITS+QRA) and a conditional Normalizing-Flow forecaster (NF) are compared with two TSFMs, namely Moirai and ChronosX. On the one hand, we find that TSFMs outperform task-specific deep learning models trained from scratch in terms of CRPS, Energy Score, and predictive interval calibration across market conditions. On the other hand, we find that well-configured task-specific models, particularly NHITS combined with QRA, achieve performance very close to TSFMs, and in some scenarios, such as when supplied with additional informative feature groups or adapted via few-shot learning from other European markets, they can even surpass TSFMs. Overall, our findings show that while TSFMs offer expressive modeling capabilities, conventional models remain highly competitive, emphasizing the need to weigh computational expense against marginal performance improvements in PEPF.

AI.tldr: TSFMs outperform task-specific models in probabilistic electricity price forecasting, but well-configured conventional models can be competitive and sometimes better, especially with additional features or few-shot learning.
AI.motivation: Large-scale renewable energy introduces volatility into electricity systems, making accurate probabilistic price forecasting essential for operational decisions, risk reduction, and market efficiency. It's unclear whether to use task-specific ML models or Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) for this purpose.
AI.method: The paper compares four models for day-ahead probabilistic EPF in European bidding zones: two task-specific models (NHITS with Quantile-Regression Averaging and conditional Normalizing-Flow forecaster) and two TSFMs (Moirai and ChronosX). Evaluation metrics include CRPS, Energy Score, and predictive interval calibration.
AI.result: TSFMs generally outperform task-specific deep learning models across market conditions in terms of CRPS, Energy Score, and calibration. However, well-configured task-specific models (particularly NHITS+QRA) achieve performance very close to TSFMs, and can sometimes surpass them when provided with additional informative features or adapted via few-shot learning from other markets.
AI.conclusion: While TSFMs offer strong modeling capabilities, conventional task-specific models remain highly competitive. The decision should consider computational costs versus marginal performance improvements, with well-configured traditional approaches being viable alternatives.

title: Personalized and Context-Aware Transformer Models for Predicting Post-Intervention Physiological Responses from Wearable Sensor Data
summary: Consumer wearables enable continuous measurement of physiological data related to stress and recovery, but turning these streams into actionable, personalized stress-management recommendations remains a challenge. In practice, users often do not know how a given intervention, defined as an activity intended to reduce stress, will affect heart rate (HR), heart rate variability (HRV), or inter-beat intervals (BBI) over the next 15 to 120 minutes. We present a framework that predicts post-intervention trajectories and the direction of change for these physiological indicators across time windows. Our methodology combines a Transformer model for multi-horizon trajectories of percent change relative to a pre-intervention baseline, direction-of-change calls (positive, negative, or neutral) at each horizon, and an empirical study using wearable sensor data overlaid with user-tagged events and interventions. This proof of concept shows that personalized post-intervention prediction is feasible. We encourage future integration into stress-management tools for personalized intervention recommendations tailored to each person's day following further validation in larger studies and, where applicable, appropriate regulatory review.

AI.tldr: A framework using Transformer models predicts personalized physiological responses to stress interventions from wearable data, enabling actionable stress-management recommendations.
AI.motivation: While consumer wearables provide continuous physiological data related to stress and recovery, users lack actionable insights about how specific interventions will affect their heart rate, heart rate variability, and inter-beat intervals over time, preventing effective personalized stress management.
AI.method: The framework combines a Transformer model to predict multi-horizon trajectories (15-120 minutes) of percent change in physiological indicators relative to pre-intervention baselines, plus direction-of-change classification (positive, negative, neutral) at each time horizon, using wearable sensor data overlaid with user-tagged events and interventions.
AI.result: Proof of concept demonstrates that personalized post-intervention prediction is feasible, showing the framework can predict physiological trajectories and direction changes after interventions using wearable data.
AI.conclusion: The approach should be integrated into stress-management tools for personalized intervention recommendations after further validation in larger studies and appropriate regulatory review where applicable.

title: Wasserstein Formulation of Reinforcement Learning. An Optimal Transport Perspective on Policy Optimization
summary: We present a geometric framework for Reinforcement Learning (RL) that views policies as maps into the Wasserstein space of action probabilities. First, we define a Riemannian structure induced by stationary distributions, proving its existence in a general context. We then define the tangent space of policies and characterize the geodesics, specifically addressing the measurability of vector fields mapped from the state space to the tangent space of probability measures over the action space. Next, we formulate a general RL optimization problem and construct a gradient flow using Otto's calculus. We compute the gradient and the Hessian of the energy, providing a formal second-order analysis. Finally, we illustrate the method with numerical examples for low-dimensional problems, computing the gradient directly from our theoretical formalism. For high-dimensional problems, we parameterize the policy using a neural network and optimize it based on an ergodic approximation of the cost.

AI.tldr: A geometric framework for RL that views policies as maps into Wasserstein space, establishing Riemannian structure and gradient flow using Otto's calculus.
AI.motivation: To develop a geometric framework for Reinforcement Learning that provides a principled mathematical structure for policy optimization by viewing policies as mappings into the space of probability measures.
AI.method: 1. Define Riemannian structure induced by stationary distributions and prove its general existence. 2. Define tangent space of policies and characterize geodesics. 3. Formulate RL optimization problem and construct gradient flow using Otto's calculus. 4. Compute gradient and Hessian for second-order analysis. 5. Apply to low-dimensional problems directly and parameterize with neural networks for high-dimensional problems.
AI.result: Established a complete geometric framework with proven existence of Riemannian structure, characterized geodesics in policy space, derived gradient and Hessian computations, and demonstrated applicability through numerical examples for both low-dimensional and high-dimensional problems.
AI.conclusion: The geometric framework provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for RL policy optimization, enabling gradient flows and second-order analysis in Wasserstein space, with practical applications ranging from direct computation for low-dimensional problems to neural network parameterization for high-dimensional ones.

title: Disentangle-then-Refine: LLM-Guided Decoupling and Structure-Aware Refinement for Graph Contrastive Learning
summary: Conventional Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) on Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) relies on blind stochastic augmentations, inadvertently entangling task-relevant signals with noise. We propose SDM-SCR, a robust framework anchored in Approximate Orthogonal Decomposition. First, the Semantic Decoupling Module (SDM) leverages the instruction-following capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to actively parse raw attributes into asymmetric, task-oriented signal and noise views. This shifts the paradigm from random perturbation to semantic-aware disentanglement. Subsequently, Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) exploits the spectral observation that semantic signals are topologically smooth while residual noise is high-frequency. SCR functions as a selective spectral filter, enforcing consistency only on the signal subspace to eliminate LLM hallucinations without over-smoothing. This ``Disentangle-then-Refine'' mechanism ensures rigorous signal purification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SDM-SCR achieves SOTA performance in accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: SDM-SCR is a new Graph Contrastive Learning framework for Text-Attributed Graphs that uses LLMs to semantically disentangle task-relevant signals from noise, then applies spectral filtering to refine signals while preventing over-smoothing.
AI.motivation: Conventional GCL methods on TAGs use random augmentations that mix task-relevant signals with irrelevant noise, leading to suboptimal learning. There's a need for a more deliberate approach to separate meaningful semantic signals from noise.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Semantic Decoupling Module (SDM) uses LLMs' instruction-following capability to parse raw text attributes into task-oriented signal and noise views (asymmetric decomposition). 2) Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) applies spectral filtering based on the observation that semantic signals are topologically smooth (low-frequency) while noise is high-frequency, enforcing consistency only on the signal subspace.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that SDM-SCR achieves state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed "Disentangle-then-Refine" mechanism through SDM-SCR provides a robust framework for GCL on TAGs, enabling rigorous signal purification by moving from random perturbation to semantic-aware disentanglement and selective spectral filtering.

title: Constraint-based Pre-training: From Structured Constraints to Scalable Model Initialization
summary: The pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm has become the dominant approach for model adaptation. However, conventional pre-training typically yields models at a fixed scale, whereas practical deployment often requires models of varying sizes, exposing its limitations when target model scales differ from those used during pre-training. To address this, we propose an innovative constraint-based pre-training paradigm that imposes structured constraints during pre-training to disentangle size-agnostic knowledge into reusable weight templates, while assigning size-specific adaptation to lightweight weight scalers, thereby reformulating variable-sized model initialization as a multi-task adaptation problem. Within this paradigm, we further introduce WeiT, which employs Kronecker-based constraints to regularize the pre-training process. Specifically, model parameters are represented as compositions of weight templates via concatenation and weighted aggregation, with adaptive connections governed by lightweight weight scalers whose parameters are learned from limited data. This design enables flexible and efficient construction of model weights across diverse downstream scales. Extensive experiments demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of WeiT, achieving state-of-the-art performance in initializing models with varying depths and widths across a broad range of perception and embodied learning tasks, including Image Classification, Image Generation, and Embodied Control. Moreover, its effectiveness generalizes to both Transformer-based and Convolution-based architectures, consistently enabling faster convergence and improved performance even under full training.

AI.tldr: WeiT is a constraint-based pre-training method that uses Kronecker-based constraints to create reusable weight templates and lightweight weight scalers, enabling flexible initialization of models at varying sizes across different architectures and tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional pre-training produces models at fixed scales, but practical deployment requires models of varying sizes. This creates limitations when target model scales differ from those used during pre-training, necessitating a more flexible approach to model initialization.
AI.method: Proposes a constraint-based pre-training paradigm that imposes structured constraints to disentangle size-agnostic knowledge into reusable weight templates. WeiT specifically employs Kronecker-based constraints where model parameters are composed of weight templates via concatenation and weighted aggregation, governed by lightweight weight scalers learned from limited data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show WeiT achieves state-of-the-art performance in initializing models with varying depths and widths across perception and embodied learning tasks (Image Classification, Image Generation, Embodied Control). The method enables faster convergence and improved performance even under full training, and generalizes to both Transformer-based and Convolution-based architectures.
AI.conclusion: WeiT provides an effective solution to the scale mismatch problem in pre-training by reformulating variable-sized model initialization as a multi-task adaptation problem. The approach demonstrates strong performance across diverse tasks and architectures while maintaining efficiency and flexibility in model construction.

title: CoTEvol: Self-Evolving Chain-of-Thoughts for Data Synthesis in Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong mathematical reasoning when trained on high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) that articulates intermediate steps, yet costly CoT curation hinders further progress. While existing remedies such as distillation from stronger LLMs and self-synthesis based on test-time search alleviate this issue, they often suffer from diminishing returns or high computing overhead.In this work, we propose CoTEvol, a genetic evolutionary framework that casts CoT generation as a population-based search over reasoning trajectories.Candidate trajectories are iteratively evolved through reflective global crossover at the trajectory level and local mutation guided by uncertainty at the step level, enabling holistic recombination and fine-grained refinement. Lightweight, task-aware fitness functions are designed to guide the evolutionary process toward accurate and diverse reasoning. Empirically, CoTEvol improves correct-CoT synthesis success by over 30% and enhances structural diversity, with markedly improved efficiency. LLMs trained on these evolutionary CoT data achieve an average gain of 6.6% across eight math benchmarks, outperforming previous distillation and self-synthesis approaches. These results underscore the promise of evolutionary CoT synthesis as a scalable and effective method for mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: CoTEvol is a genetic evolutionary framework that generates diverse and accurate Chain-of-Thought reasoning trajectories through population-based search, achieving significant improvements in CoT synthesis success and downstream mathematical reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for generating high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning face limitations: manual curation is costly, distillation from stronger LLMs shows diminishing returns, and self-synthesis methods incur high computing overhead, hindering progress in mathematical reasoning with LLMs.
AI.method: CoTEvol treats CoT generation as a population-based search over reasoning trajectories. It evolves candidate trajectories through reflective global crossover at the trajectory level and local mutation guided by uncertainty at the step level. Lightweight, task-aware fitness functions guide the evolutionary process toward accurate and diverse reasoning.
AI.result: CoTEvol improves correct-CoT synthesis success by over 30% and enhances structural diversity with improved efficiency. LLMs trained on evolutionary CoT data achieve an average gain of 6.6% across eight math benchmarks, outperforming previous distillation and self-synthesis approaches.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary CoT synthesis is a scalable and effective method for mathematical reasoning tasks, demonstrating significant improvements in CoT quality, diversity, and downstream performance compared to existing approaches.

title: MirrorBench: Evaluating Self-centric Intelligence in MLLMs by Introducing a Mirror
summary: Recent progress in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has demonstrated remarkable advances in perception and reasoning, suggesting their potential for embodied intelligence. While recent studies have evaluated embodied MLLMs in interactive settings, current benchmarks mainly target capabilities to perceive, understand, and interact with external objects, lacking a systematic evaluation of self-centric intelligence. To address this, we introduce MirrorBench, a simulation-based benchmark inspired by the classical Mirror Self-Recognition (MSR) test in psychology. MirrorBench extends this paradigm to embodied MLLMs through a tiered framework of progressively challenging tasks, assessing agents from basic visual perception to high-level self-representation. Experiments on leading MLLMs show that even at the lowest level, their performance remains substantially inferior to human performance, revealing fundamental limitations in self-referential understanding. Our study bridges psychological paradigms and embodied intelligence, offering a principled framework for evaluating the emergence of general intelligence in large models. Project page: https://fflahm.github.io/mirror-bench-page/.

AI.tldr: MirrorBench is a simulation-based benchmark for evaluating self-centric intelligence in embodied MLLMs through mirror self-recognition tasks.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for embodied Multimodal Large Language Models focus on external object interaction but lack systematic evaluation of self-centric intelligence and self-referential understanding.
AI.method: Developed MirrorBench, inspired by psychological Mirror Self-Recognition test, featuring tiered framework with progressively challenging tasks from basic visual perception to high-level self-representation in simulated environments.
AI.result: Experiments show leading MLLMs perform substantially worse than humans even at the lowest task levels, revealing fundamental limitations in self-referential understanding.
AI.conclusion: The study bridges psychological paradigms with embodied intelligence evaluation and provides a principled framework for assessing the emergence of general intelligence in large models.

title: Adaptive Test-Time Compute Allocation for Reasoning LLMs via Constrained Policy Optimization
summary: Test-time compute scaling, the practice of spending extra computation during inference via repeated sampling, search, or extended reasoning, has become a powerful lever for improving large language model performance. Yet deploying these techniques under finite inference budgets requires a decision that current systems largely ignore: which inputs deserve more compute, and which can be answered cheaply? We formalize this as a constrained optimization problem (maximize expected accuracy subject to an average compute budget) and solve it with a two-stage Solve-then-Learn pipeline. In the solve stage, Lagrangian relaxation decomposes the global constraint into per-instance sub-problems, each admitting a closed-form oracle action that optimally prices accuracy against cost. We prove that the induced cost is monotone in the dual variable, enabling exact budget targeting via binary search. In the learn stage, a lightweight classifier is trained to predict oracle actions from cheap input features, amortizing the allocation rule for real-time deployment. We establish that the task-level regret of the learned policy is bounded by its imitation error times the worst-case per-instance gap, yielding a clean reduction from constrained inference to supervised classification. Experiments on MATH and GSM8K with three LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen2.5-7B) show that our method consistently outperforms uniform and heuristic allocation baselines, achieving up to 12.8% relative accuracy improvement on MATH under matched budget constraints, while closely tracking the Lagrangian oracle upper bound with over 91% imitation accuracy.

AI.tldr: Test-time compute allocation optimization that uses Lagrangian relaxation to optimally allocate inference compute across inputs under budget constraints, achieving up to 12.8% accuracy improvement on MATH benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM systems ignore the critical decision of allocating inference compute across inputs under finite budgets, leading to suboptimal performance when some inputs deserve more compute than others.
AI.method: Two-stage Solve-then-Learn pipeline: 1) Solve stage uses Lagrangian relaxation to decompose global budget constraint into per-instance sub-problems with closed-form oracle actions, 2) Learn stage trains lightweight classifier to predict oracle actions from cheap features for real-time deployment.
AI.result: Method outperforms uniform and heuristic allocation baselines, achieving up to 12.8% relative accuracy improvement on MATH under matched budget constraints, with over 91% imitation accuracy tracking the Lagrangian oracle upper bound.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled solution for compute allocation in LLMs, reducing the constrained inference problem to supervised classification while enabling real-time deployment with guaranteed regret bounds.

title: CogEvolution: A Human-like Generative Educational Agent to Simulate Student's Cognitive Evolution
summary: Generative Agents, owing to their precise modeling and simulation capabilities of human behavior, have become a pivotal tool in the field of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIEd) for uncovering complex cognitive processes of learners. However, existing educational agents predominantly rely on static personas to simulate student learning behaviors, neglecting the decisive role of deep cognitive capabilities in learning outcomes during practice interactions. Furthermore, they struggle to characterize the dynamic fluidity of knowledge internalization, transfer, and cognitive state transitions. To overcome this bottleneck, this paper proposes a human-like educational agent capable of simulating student cognitive evolution: CogEvolution. Specifically, we first construct a cognitive depth perceptron based on the Interactive, Constructive, Active, Passive (ICAP) taxonomy from cognitive psychology, achieving precise quantification of learner cognitive engagement. Subsequently, we propose a memory retrieval method based on Item Response Theory (IRT) to simulate the connection and assimilation of new and prior knowledge. Finally, we design a dynamic cognitive update mechanism based on evolutionary algorithms to simulate the real-time integration of student learning behaviors and cognitive evolution processes. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that CogEvolution not only significantly outperforms baseline models in behavioral fidelity and learning curve fitting but also uniquely reproduces plausible and robust cognitive evolutionary paths consistent with educational psychology expectations, providing a novel paradigm for constructing highly interpretable educational agents.

AI.tldr: CogEvolution is an AI educational agent that simulates student cognitive evolution using cognitive depth perceptron (ICAP), IRT-based memory retrieval, and evolutionary algorithm updates, outperforming existing static persona-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing educational agents use static personas that neglect deep cognitive capabilities' role in learning outcomes and fail to capture dynamic knowledge internalization and cognitive state transitions, limiting their ability to simulate realistic student learning evolution.
AI.method: 1. Construct cognitive depth perceptron based on ICAP taxonomy for precise quantification of cognitive engagement; 2. Propose memory retrieval method using Item Response Theory (IRT) to simulate new-prior knowledge connections; 3. Design dynamic cognitive update mechanism using evolutionary algorithms to integrate learning behaviors with cognitive evolution.
AI.result: CogEvolution significantly outperforms baseline models in behavioral fidelity and learning curve fitting, and uniquely reproduces plausible, robust cognitive evolutionary paths consistent with educational psychology expectations.
AI.conclusion: CogEvolution provides a novel paradigm for constructing highly interpretable educational agents by overcoming limitations of static personas and successfully simulating human-like cognitive evolution in learning processes.

title: Curvature-Aligned Probing for Local Loss-Landscape Stabilization
summary: Local loss-landscape stabilization under sample growth is typically measured either pointwise or through isotropic averaging in the full parameter space. Despite practical value, both choices probe directions that contribute little to the dominant local deformation of strongly anisotropic neural landscapes. We recast stabilization as an observational problem and introduce a unified family of criteria parameterized by an aggregation order and a probing distribution; within this family we propose a curvature-aligned criterion $Δ_2^{(D)}$ that probes the loss increment field in the top-$D$ eigenspace of the empirical Hessian near a trained solution. Solely from a local quadratic model, we prove that $Δ_2^{(D)}$ preserves the $O(k^{-2})$ mean-squared rate of the full-space criterion while replacing ambient-dimension curvature dependence with dependence on the subspace dimension $D$; a corollary gives a closed-form spectral expression and a proposition identifies the top-$D$ eigenspace as extremal within the eigenspace-aligned family. We also derive scalable estimators based on Hessian-vector products, subspace Monte Carlo, and a closed-form Gaussian-moment proxy. On a decoder-only transformer, a curvature-aligned probe occupying a tiny fraction of parameter space already reproduces the full-space mean-squared signal to within numerical noise throughout the validated local regime, and the closed-form estimator is orders of magnitude faster than direct Monte Carlo after subspace construction.

AI.tldr: Proposes a curvature-aligned criterion for measuring local loss landscape stabilization that probes only the top eigenspace of the empirical Hessian, reducing computational cost while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for measuring loss landscape stabilization either use pointwise measurements or isotropic averaging, both of which probe directions that contribute little to dominant local deformations in strongly anisotropic neural landscapes.
AI.method: Introduces a family of stabilization criteria parameterized by aggregation order and probing distribution, proposing Δ₂⁽ᴰ⁾ which probes loss increments in the top-D eigenspace of the empirical Hessian. Derives scalable estimators using Hessian-vector products, subspace Monte Carlo, and a closed-form Gaussian-moment proxy.
AI.result: The curvature-aligned criterion preserves the O(k⁻²) mean-squared rate of full-space criterion while replacing ambient-dimension curvature dependence with subspace dimension D dependence. On a decoder-only transformer, the method using a tiny fraction of parameter space reproduces full-space signal within numerical noise, with closed-form estimator being orders of magnitude faster.
AI.conclusion: Probing the top eigenspace of the Hessian provides an efficient and accurate way to measure loss landscape stabilization, dramatically reducing computational requirements while maintaining accuracy compared to full-space approaches.

title: Sequence Search: Automated Sequence Design using Neural Architecture Search
summary: Developing an MR sequence is challenging and remains largely constrained by human intuition. Recently, AI-driven approaches have been proposed; however, most require an initial sequence for parameter optimization or extensive training datasets, limiting their general applicability. In this study, we propose "Sequence Search," an automated sequence design framework based on neural architecture search. The method takes tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives as inputs and generates pulse sequences satisfying the design objectives, without requiring prior knowledge of conventional sequence structures. Sequence Search iteratively generates candidate sequences through neural architecture search and optimizes them via a differentiable Bloch simulator and objective-specific loss functions using gradient-based learning. The framework successfully replicated conventional spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, and inversion recovery sequences. Less intuitive solutions were also discovered, such as three-RF spin-echo-like sequences with reduced RF energy and refocusing phases deviating from the conventional Hahn-echo. This work establishes a generalizable framework for automated MR sequence design, highlighting the potential to explore configurations beyond conventional designs based on human intuition.

AI.tldr: An automated MRI sequence design framework called "Sequence Search" uses neural architecture search to generate pulse sequences from tissue properties and design objectives without requiring prior knowledge of conventional sequences.
AI.motivation: MR sequence development is challenging and constrained by human intuition. Existing AI approaches require initial sequences or extensive training data, limiting general applicability. There's a need for an automated framework that can design sequences from basic inputs without human preconceptions.
AI.method: A neural architecture search-based framework that takes tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives as inputs. It iteratively generates candidate sequences and optimizes them via a differentiable Bloch simulator and objective-specific loss functions using gradient-based learning.
AI.result: The framework successfully replicated conventional sequences (spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, inversion recovery) and discovered novel sequences like three-RF spin-echo-like sequences with reduced RF energy and unconventional refocusing phases.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a generalizable framework for automated MR sequence design that can explore configurations beyond conventional human-intuition-based designs, demonstrating potential for discovering more efficient or novel imaging sequences.

title: Does RL Expand the Capability Boundary of LLM Agents? A PASS@(k,T) Analysis
summary: Does reinforcement learning genuinely expand what LLM agents can do, or merely make them more reliable? For static reasoning, recent work answers the second: base and RL pass@k curves converge at large k. We ask whether this holds for agentic tool use, where T rounds of interaction enable compositional strategies that re-sampling cannot recover. We introduce PASS@(k,T), a two-dimensional metric that jointly varies sampling budget k and interaction depth T, separating capability expansion from efficiency improvement. Our main finding is that, contrary to the static-reasoning result, tool-use RL genuinely enlarges the capability boundary: the RL agent's pass-curve pulls above the base model's and the gap widens at large k rather than converging. The expansion is specific to compositional, sequential information gathering; on simpler tasks RL behaves as prior work predicts. Under matched training data, supervised fine-tuning regresses the boundary on the same compositional tasks, isolating self-directed exploration as the causal factor. Mechanism analysis shows RL reweights the base strategy distribution toward the subset whose downstream reasoning more often yields a correct answer, with the improvement concentrated on how the agent integrates retrieved information. These results reconcile optimistic and pessimistic readings of RL for LLMs: both are correct, on different task types.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning genuinely expands LLM agent capabilities for compositional tool-use tasks, unlike static reasoning where RL merely improves efficiency.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether RL for LLM agents genuinely expands their capabilities or just makes them more reliable. While previous work showed RL only improves efficiency for static reasoning (converging curves at large k), the authors question if this holds for agentic tool use where compositional strategies across multiple interactions can't be recovered by simple re-sampling.
AI.method: Introduced PASS@(k,T), a two-dimensional metric varying sampling budget (k) and interaction depth (T) to separate capability expansion from efficiency improvement. Compared RL agents against base models on compositional tool-use tasks, analyzed under matched training data, and used supervised fine-tuning as a control to isolate the causal factor.
AI.result: Contrary to static-reasoning findings, tool-use RL genuinely enlarges capability boundaries: RL agent's pass-curve pulls above base model's and the gap widens at large k rather than converging. The expansion is specific to compositional, sequential information gathering tasks. Under matched data, supervised fine-tuning regresses the boundary, isolating self-directed exploration as causal factor. RL reweights base strategy distribution toward subsets whose downstream reasoning yields correct answers, with improvement concentrated on information integration.
AI.conclusion: Both optimistic and pessimistic readings of RL for LLMs are correct but on different task types: RL genuinely expands capabilities for compositional tool-use tasks while only improving efficiency for static reasoning tasks.

title: A Comparative Study of CNN Optimization Methods for Edge AI: Exploring the Role of Early Exits
summary: Deploying deep neural networks on edge devices requires balancing accuracy, latency, and resource constraints under realistic execution conditions. To fit models within these constraints, two broad strategies have emerged: static compression techniques such as pruning and quantization, which permanently reduce model size, and dynamic approaches such as early-exit mechanisms, which adapt computational cost at runtime. While both families are widely studied in isolation, they are rarely compared under identical conditions on physical hardware. This paper presents a unified deployment-oriented comparison of static compression and dynamic early-exit mechanisms, evaluated on real edge devices using ONNX based inference pipelines. Our results show that static and dynamic techniques offer fundamentally different trade-offs for edge deployment. While pruning and quantization deliver consistent memory footprint reduction, early-exit mechanisms enable input-adaptive computation savings that static methods cannot match. Their combination proves highly effective, simultaneously reducing inference latency and memory usage with minimal accuracy loss, expanding what is achievable at the edge.

AI.tldr: The paper compares static compression (pruning/quantization) with dynamic early-exit mechanisms for neural network deployment on edge devices, showing they offer different trade-offs but their combination is highly effective.
AI.motivation: Edge deployment requires balancing accuracy, latency, and resource constraints under realistic conditions. While both static compression and dynamic early-exit approaches exist, they are rarely compared under identical conditions on physical hardware.
AI.method: The authors conduct a unified deployment-oriented comparison of static compression and dynamic early-exit mechanisms, evaluating them on real edge devices using ONNX-based inference pipelines under identical conditions.
AI.result: Static methods (pruning/quantization) deliver consistent memory footprint reduction, while early-exit mechanisms enable input-adaptive computation savings. Their combination proves highly effective, simultaneously reducing inference latency and memory usage with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: Static and dynamic techniques offer fundamentally different but complementary trade-offs for edge deployment. Combining them expands what is achievable at the edge by simultaneously addressing both computational cost and memory constraints.

title: SOLIS: Physics-Informed Learning of Interpretable Neural Surrogates for Nonlinear Systems
comment: in the International Joint Conference on Neural Networks, 2026
summary: Nonlinear system identification must balance physical interpretability with model flexibility. Classical methods yield structured, control-relevant models but rely on rigid parametric forms that often miss complex nonlinearities, whereas Neural ODEs are expressive yet largely black-box. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) sit between these extremes, but inverse PINNs typically assume a known governing equation with fixed coefficients, leading to identifiability failures when the true dynamics are unknown or state-dependent. We propose \textbf{SOLIS}, which models unknown dynamics via a \emph{state-conditioned second-order surrogate model} and recasts identification as learning a Quasi-Linear Parameter-Varying (Quasi-LPV) representation, recovering interpretable natural frequency, damping, and gain without presupposing a global equation. SOLIS decouples trajectory reconstruction from parameter estimation and stabilizes training with a cyclic curriculum and \textbf{Local Physics Hints} windowed ridge-regression anchors that mitigate optimization collapse. Experiments on benchmarks show accurate parameter-manifold recovery and coherent physical rollouts from sparse data, including regimes where standard inverse methods fail.

AI.tldr: SOLIS proposes a novel nonlinear system identification approach using state-conditioned second-order surrogate models to learn Quasi-LPV representations, enabling interpretable parameter recovery without assuming global governing equations.
AI.motivation: Current nonlinear system identification methods face a trade-off: classical parametric models are interpretable but lack flexibility, while Neural ODEs/PINNs are expressive but black-box or require known governing equations. Inverse PINNs fail when dynamics are unknown or state-dependent.
AI.method: SOLIS models unknown dynamics via state-conditioned second-order surrogate models and recasts identification as learning Quasi-Linear Parameter-Varying representations. It uses cyclic curriculum training and Local Physics Hints (windowed ridge-regression anchors) to stabilize optimization and prevent collapse.
AI.result: Experiments show accurate recovery of parameter manifolds (natural frequency, damping, gain) and coherent physical rollouts from sparse data, outperforming standard inverse methods in challenging regimes.
AI.conclusion: SOLIS bridges the gap between interpretability and flexibility in nonlinear system identification, enabling physics-informed learning without presupposing global equations, with robust performance even where traditional methods fail.

title: Diffusion Crossover: Defining Evolutionary Recombination in Diffusion Models via Noise Sequence Interpolation
summary: Interactive Evolutionary Computation (IEC) provides a powerful framework for optimizing subjective criteria such as human preferences and aesthetics, yet it suffers from a fundamental limitation: in high-dimensional generative representations, defining crossover in a semantically consistent manner is difficult, often leading to a mutation-dominated search. In this work, we explicitly define crossover in diffusion models. We propose Diffusion crossover, which formulates evolutionary recombination as step-wise interpolation of noise sequences in the reverse process of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). By applying spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) to the noise sequences associated with selected parent images, the proposed method generates offspring that inherit characteristics from both parents while preserving the geometric structure of the diffusion process. Furthermore, controlling the time-step range of interpolation enables a principled trade-off between diversity (exploration) and convergence (exploitation). Experimental results using PCA analysis and perceptual similarity metrics (LPIPS) demonstrate that Diffusion crossover produces perceptually smooth and semantically consistent transitions between parent images. Qualitative interactive evolution experiments further confirm that the proposed method effectively supports human-in-the-loop image exploration. These findings suggest a new perspective: diffusion models are not only powerful generators, but also structured evolutionary search spaces in which recombination can be explicitly defined and controlled.

AI.tldr: Diffusion crossover enables evolutionary recombination in diffusion models via step-wise noise interpolation, solving the semantic crossover problem in interactive evolutionary computation for image generation.
AI.motivation: Interactive Evolutionary Computation struggles with semantic crossover in high-dimensional generative representations (like diffusion models), often relying too much on mutation. There's a need to define consistent crossover operations for diffusion models to enable better evolutionary search.
AI.method: Proposes Diffusion crossover by formulating evolutionary recombination as step-wise interpolation of noise sequences in the reverse process of DDPMs. Uses spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) on noise sequences of parent images to generate offspring, with controlled time-step range for exploration/exploitation trade-off.
AI.result: Experiments show Diffusion crossover produces perceptually smooth and semantically consistent transitions between parent images (verified via PCA and LPIPS metrics). Qualitative interactive evolution experiments confirm effective human-in-the-loop image exploration support.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models serve not only as powerful generators but also as structured evolutionary search spaces where recombination can be explicitly defined and controlled, advancing Interactive Evolutionary Computation capabilities.

title: xFODE+: Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs for Uncertainty Quantification
comment: in IEEE International Conference on Fuzzy Systems, 2026
summary: Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL) have boosted data-driven System Identification (SysID), but reliable use requires Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) alongside accurate predictions.
