Although UQ-capable models such as Fuzzy ODE (FODE) can produce Prediction Intervals (PIs), they offer limited interpretability. We introduce Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs for UQ (xFODE+), an interpretable SysID model which produces PIs alongside point predictions while retaining physically meaningful incremental states. xFODE+ implements each fuzzy additive model with Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic Systems (IT2-FLSs) and constraints membership functions to the activation of two neighboring rules, limiting overlap and keeping inference locally transparent. The type-reduced sets produced by the IT2-FLSs are aggregated to construct the state update together with the PIs. The model is trained in a DL framework via a composite loss that jointly optimizes prediction accuracy and PI quality. Results on benchmark SysID datasets show that xFODE+ matches FODE in PI quality and achieves comparable accuracy, while providing interpretability.

AI.tldr: xFODE+ is an interpretable deep learning model for system identification that provides both point predictions and prediction intervals while maintaining physical interpretability through constrained fuzzy logic systems.
AI.motivation: While deep learning has improved data-driven system identification, reliable deployment requires uncertainty quantification alongside accurate predictions. Existing uncertainty-capable models like Fuzzy ODE can produce prediction intervals but lack interpretability, making it difficult to understand their internal workings and decisions.
AI.method: xFODE+ uses Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs with Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic Systems. The model constrains membership functions to the activation of only two neighboring rules to limit overlap and maintain local transparency. Type-reduced sets from IT2-FLSs are aggregated to construct state updates and prediction intervals, trained via a composite loss function optimizing both accuracy and prediction interval quality in a deep learning framework.
AI.result: On benchmark system identification datasets, xFODE+ achieves comparable prediction accuracy to FODE while matching its prediction interval quality, but with the added benefit of providing interpretability that FODE lacks.
AI.conclusion: xFODE+ successfully addresses the interpretability gap in uncertainty-aware system identification models by combining the uncertainty quantification capabilities of fuzzy ODEs with transparent, locally interpretable fuzzy logic systems, making it suitable for applications where both reliable predictions and model understanding are important.

title: The LLM Fallacy: Misattribution in AI-Assisted Cognitive Workflows
summary: The rapid integration of large language models (LLMs) into everyday workflows has transformed how individuals perform cognitive tasks such as writing, programming, analysis, and multilingual communication. While prior research has focused on model reliability, hallucination, and user trust calibration, less attention has been given to how LLM usage reshapes users' perceptions of their own capabilities. This paper introduces the LLM fallacy, a cognitive attribution error in which individuals misinterpret LLM-assisted outputs as evidence of their own independent competence, producing a systematic divergence between perceived and actual capability. We argue that the opacity, fluency, and low-friction interaction patterns of LLMs obscure the boundary between human and machine contribution, leading users to infer competence from outputs rather than from the processes that generate them. We situate the LLM fallacy within existing literature on automation bias, cognitive offloading, and human--AI collaboration, while distinguishing it as a form of attributional distortion specific to AI-mediated workflows. We propose a conceptual framework of its underlying mechanisms and a typology of manifestations across computational, linguistic, analytical, and creative domains. Finally, we examine implications for education, hiring, and AI literacy, and outline directions for empirical validation. We also provide a transparent account of human--AI collaborative methodology. This work establishes a foundation for understanding how generative AI systems not only augment cognitive performance but also reshape self-perception and perceived expertise.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the "LLM fallacy" - a cognitive attribution error where users misattribute AI-assisted outputs to their own competence, creating a gap between perceived and actual capability.
AI.motivation: Despite research on LLM reliability and trust, little attention has been given to how LLM usage affects users' self-perception of their own capabilities, particularly how it distorts their assessment of personal competence.
AI.method: The paper proposes a conceptual framework examining the LLM fallacy's underlying mechanisms, develops a typology of manifestations across domains (computational, linguistic, analytical, creative), and outlines empirical validation directions while providing a transparent account of human-AI collaborative methodology.
AI.result: The study establishes that LLMs not only augment cognitive performance but systematically reshape self-perception and perceived expertise through attributional distortions specific to AI-mediated workflows.
AI.conclusion: The LLM fallacy represents a significant cognitive distortion with implications for education, hiring, and AI literacy, requiring empirical investigation and mitigation strategies as generative AI systems become more integrated into daily cognitive tasks.

title: xFODE: An Explainable Fuzzy Additive ODE Framework for System Identification
comment: in IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2026
summary: Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL) have strengthened data-driven System Identification (SysID), with Neural and Fuzzy Ordinary Differential Equation (NODE/FODE) models achieving high accuracy in nonlinear dynamic modeling. Yet, system states in these frameworks are often reconstructed without clear physical meaning, and input contributions to the state derivatives remain difficult to interpret. To address these limitations, we propose Explainable FODE (xFODE), an interpretable SysID framework with integrated DL-based training. In xFODE, we define states in an incremental form to provide them with physical meanings. We employ fuzzy additive models to approximate the state derivative, thereby enhancing interpretability per input. To provide further interpretability, Partitioning Strategies (PSs) are developed, enabling the training of fuzzy additive models with explainability. By structuring the antecedent space during training so that only two consecutive rules are activated for any given input, PSs not only yield lower complexity for local inference but also enhance the interpretability of the antecedent space. To train xFODE, we present a DL framework with parameterized membership function learning that supports end-to-end optimization. Across benchmark SysID datasets, xFODE matches the accuracy of NODE, FODE, and NLARX models while providing interpretable insights.

AI.tldr: xFODE is an interpretable system identification framework that uses fuzzy additive models with incremental state definitions and partitioning strategies to achieve NODE-level accuracy while providing physical meaning and interpretability to system states and inputs.
AI.motivation: Current data-driven system identification methods (NODE/FODE) achieve high accuracy but lack interpretability - system states often have unclear physical meaning, and input contributions to state derivatives are difficult to interpret, limiting practical understanding and trust.
AI.method: Proposes Explainable FODE (xFODE) with: 1) incremental state definitions for physical meaning, 2) fuzzy additive models for approximating state derivatives with per-input interpretability, 3) partitioning strategies that ensure only two consecutive fuzzy rules activate for any input, simplifying inference, 4) deep learning framework with parameterized membership functions for end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: xFODE matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art models (NODE, FODE, NLARX) on benchmark system identification datasets while providing interpretable insights into system behavior and input contributions.
AI.conclusion: xFODE successfully bridges the gap between accuracy and interpretability in data-driven system identification, offering a framework that maintains NODE-level performance while providing physically meaningful states and interpretable input contributions through its novel incremental formulation and partitioning strategies.

title: Beyond Literal Summarization: Redefining Hallucination for Medical SOAP Note Evaluation
summary:
Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for clinical documentation tasks such as SOAP note generation remains challenging. Unlike standard summarization, these tasks require clinical abstraction, normalization of colloquial language, and medically grounded inference. However, prevailing evaluation methods including automated metrics and LLM as judge frameworks rely on lexical faithfulness, often labeling any information not explicitly present in the transcript as hallucination.
  We show that such approaches systematically misclassify clinically valid outputs as errors, inflating hallucination rates and distorting model assessment. Our analysis reveals that many flagged hallucinations correspond to legitimate clinical transformations, including synonym mapping, abstraction of examination findings, diagnostic inference, and guideline consistent care planning.
  By aligning evaluation criteria with clinical reasoning through calibrated prompting and retrieval grounded in medical ontologies we observe a significant shift in outcomes. Under a lexical evaluation regime, the mean hallucination rate is 35%, heavily penalizing valid reasoning. With inference aware evaluation, this drops to 9%, with remaining cases reflecting genuine safety concerns. These findings suggest that current evaluation practices over penalize valid clinical reasoning and may measure artifacts of evaluation design rather than true errors, underscoring the need for clinically informed evaluation in high context domains like medicine.

AI.tldr: Current LLM evaluation methods for clinical documentation over-penalize valid clinical reasoning as hallucinations. Using clinically-informed evaluation reduces hallucination rates from 35% to 9%, showing current methods measure evaluation artifacts rather than true errors.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for LLMs in clinical documentation tasks (like SOAP note generation) rely on lexical faithfulness metrics that label clinically valid transformations as hallucinations. This systematic misclassification inflates hallucination rates and distorts model assessment, failing to account for necessary clinical abstraction and reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose aligning evaluation criteria with clinical reasoning through calibrated prompting and retrieval grounded in medical ontologies. They compare traditional lexical evaluation methods with their inference-aware evaluation approach for assessing LLM performance on clinical documentation tasks.
AI.result: Under lexical evaluation, the mean hallucination rate is 35%, heavily penalizing valid clinical reasoning. With inference-aware evaluation, this drops to 9%, with remaining cases reflecting genuine safety concerns. Many flagged hallucinations in lexical evaluation correspond to legitimate clinical transformations including synonym mapping, abstraction of examination findings, diagnostic inference, and guideline-consistent care planning.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation practices over-penalize valid clinical reasoning and may measure artifacts of evaluation design rather than true errors. There is a critical need for clinically informed evaluation frameworks in high-context domains like medicine to accurately assess LLM performance on tasks requiring clinical abstraction and medical inference.

title: Can LLMs Score Medical Diagnoses and Clinical Reasoning as well as Expert Panels?
summary: Evaluating medical AI systems using expert clinician panels is costly and slow, motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) as alternative adjudicators. Here, we evaluate an LLM jury composed of three frontier AI models scoring 3333 diagnoses on 300 real-world middle-income country (MIC) hospital cases. Model performance was benchmarked against expert clinician panel and independent human re-scoring panel evaluations. Both LLM and clinician-generated diagnoses are scored across four dimensions: diagnosis, differential diagnosis, clinical reasoning and negative treatment risk. For each of these, we assess scoring difference, inter-rater agreement, scoring stability, severe safety errors and the effect of post-hoc calibration. We find that: (i) the uncalibrated LLM jury scores are systematically lower than clinician panels scores; (ii) the LLM Jury preserves ordinal agreement and exhibits better concordance with the primary expert panels than the human expert re-score panels do; (iii) the probability of severe errors is lower in \lj models compared to the human expert re-score panels; (iv) the LLM Jury shows excellent agreement with primary expert panels' rankings. We find that the LLM jury combined with AI model diagnoses can be used to identify ward diagnoses at high risk of error, enabling targeted expert review and improved panel efficiency; (v) LLM jury models show no self-preference bias. They did not score diagnoses generated by their own underlying model or models from the same vendor more (or less) favourably than those generated by other models. Finally, we demonstrate that LLM jury calibration using isotonic regression improves alignment with human expert panel evaluations. Together, these results provide compelling evidence that a calibrated, multi-model LLM jury can serve as a trustworthy and reliable proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking.

AI.tldr: LLM juries can reliably evaluate medical AI diagnoses, showing good agreement with expert clinicians and no bias towards their own models, with calibration improving alignment.
AI.motivation: Expert clinician panels for evaluating medical AI systems are expensive and slow, creating a need for more efficient alternatives. The researchers explore using large language models as adjudicators to reduce costs and speed up medical AI benchmarking.
AI.method: The study used a jury of three frontier AI models to score 3333 diagnoses on 300 real-world hospital cases from middle-income countries. Performance was benchmarked against expert clinician panels and independent human re-scoring panels. Both LLM and clinician-generated diagnoses were scored across four dimensions: diagnosis, differential diagnosis, clinical reasoning, and negative treatment risk. Analysis included scoring differences, inter-rater agreement, scoring stability, severe safety errors, and post-hoc calibration effects using isotonic regression.
AI.result: Uncalibrated LLM jury scores were systematically lower than clinician panel scores. The LLM jury preserved ordinal agreement and showed better concordance with primary expert panels than human expert re-score panels. Severe error probability was lower in LLM models compared to human expert re-score panels. LLM juries showed excellent agreement with primary expert panels' rankings and could identify high-risk ward diagnoses for targeted expert review. LLM models showed no self-preference bias toward their own or same-vendor models. Calibration with isotonic regression improved alignment with human expert panel evaluations.
AI.conclusion: A calibrated, multi-model LLM jury can serve as a trustworthy and reliable proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking, offering a cost-effective and efficient alternative while maintaining quality assessment standards.

title: Intermediate Layers Encode Optimal Biological Representations in Single-Cell Foundation Models
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the LMRL (Learning Meaningful Representations of Life) Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Current single-cell foundation model benchmarks universally extract final layer embeddings, assuming these represent optimal feature spaces. We systematically evaluate layer-wise representations from scFoundation (100M parameters) and Tahoe-X1 (1.3B parameters) across trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction. Our analysis reveals that optimal layers are task-dependent (trajectory peaks at 60% depth, 31% above final layers) and context-dependent (perturbation optima shift 0-96% across T cell activation states). Notably, first-layer embeddings outperform all deeper layers in quiescent cells, challenging assumptions about hierarchical feature abstraction. These findings demonstrate that "where" to extract features matters as much as "what" the model learns, necessitating systematic layer evaluation tailored to biological task and cellular context rather than defaulting to final-layer embeddings.

AI.tldr: The study reveals that optimal feature extraction layers in single-cell foundation models are task- and context-dependent, challenging the standard practice of using final-layer embeddings. First-layer embeddings can outperform deeper layers in certain biological contexts.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for single-cell foundation models universally extract final layer embeddings, assuming these represent optimal feature spaces. This assumption may not hold across different biological tasks and cellular contexts.
AI.method: The researchers systematically evaluated layer-wise representations from two large single-cell foundation models (scFoundation with 100M parameters and Tahoe-X1 with 1.3B parameters) across trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction tasks.
AI.result: Optimal layers were found to be task-dependent (trajectory inference peaks at 60% depth, 31% above final layers) and context-dependent (perturbation optima shift 0-96% across T cell activation states). Notably, first-layer embeddings outperformed all deeper layers in quiescent cells.
AI.conclusion: "Where" to extract features matters as much as "what" the model learns. The findings necessitate systematic layer evaluation tailored to biological task and cellular context rather than defaulting to final-layer embeddings.

title: Beyond Importance Sampling: Rejection-Gated Policy Optimization
comment: 27 pages, includes theoretical analysis and experiments
summary: We propose a new perspective on policy optimization: rather than reweighting all samples by their importance ratios, an optimizer should select which samples are trustworthy enough to drive a policy update. Building on this view, we introduce Rejection-Gated Policy Optimization (RGPO), which replaces the importance sampling ratio r_theta = pi_theta / pi_old with a smooth, differentiable acceptance gate alpha_theta(s, a) = g(r_theta(s, a)) in the range [0, 1]. Unlike prior work that applies rejection sampling as a data-level heuristic before training, RGPO elevates rejection to an optimization principle: the gate participates directly in gradient computation and is implicitly updated alongside the policy. RGPO provides a unified framework: the policy gradients of TRPO, PPO, and REINFORCE all correspond to specific choices of the effective gradient weight w(r) = g'(r) * r. We prove that RGPO guarantees finite, bounded gradient variance even when importance sampling ratios are heavy-tailed (where IS variance diverges). We further show that RGPO incurs only a bounded, controllable bias and provides an approximate monotonic policy improvement guarantee analogous to TRPO. RGPO matches PPO in computational cost, requires no second-order optimization, and extends naturally to RLHF-style preference alignment. In online preference fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct on Anthropic HH-RLHF (n = 3 seeds), RGPO uses a dual-ratio gate that anchors learning to both the previous policy and the reference model, achieving a Pareto-dominant outcome: the highest reward among online RL methods (+14.8% vs. PPO-RLHF) and the lowest KL divergence to the reference model (-16.0% vs. PPO-RLHF, -53.1% vs. GRPO).

AI.tldr: RGPO introduces a rejection-gated policy optimization framework that replaces importance sampling ratios with a differentiable acceptance gate, providing bounded gradient variance, controllable bias, and better performance than PPO in RLHF tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional policy optimization methods reweight all samples by importance ratios, but many samples may not be trustworthy enough to drive policy updates effectively. There's a need for a method that selectively uses trustworthy samples while avoiding the heavy-tailed variance issues of importance sampling.
AI.method: RGPO replaces the importance sampling ratio with a smooth, differentiable acceptance gate α_θ(s,a) = g(r_θ(s,a)) in [0,1]. The gate participates directly in gradient computation and is updated alongside the policy, providing a unified framework that encompasses TRPO, PPO, and REINFORCE as special cases. It uses dual-ratio gates that anchor learning to both previous policy and reference model in RLHF tasks.
AI.result: RGPO guarantees finite, bounded gradient variance even with heavy-tailed importance ratios, incurs only bounded controllable bias, and provides approximate monotonic policy improvement. In RLHF experiments on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, RGPO achieves Pareto-dominant outcomes: highest reward (+14.8% vs PPO-RLHF) and lowest KL divergence to reference model (-16.0% vs PPO-RLHF, -53.1% vs GRPO).
AI.conclusion: RGPO offers a principled approach to policy optimization by elevating rejection sampling to an optimization principle, providing better variance control, theoretical guarantees, and superior empirical performance compared to existing methods like PPO, while maintaining similar computational cost.

title: TrigReason: Trigger-Based Collaboration between Small and Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong performance on complex tasks through extended chains of thought but suffer from high inference latency due to autoregressive reasoning. Recent work explores using Small Reasoning Models (SRMs) to accelerate LRM inference. In this paper, we systematically characterize the capability boundaries of SRMs and identify three common types of reasoning risks: (1) path divergence, where SRMs lack the strategic ability to construct an initial plan, causing reasoning to deviate from the most probable path; (2) cognitive overload, where SRMs fail to solve particularly difficult steps; and (3) recovery inability, where SRMs lack robust self-reflection and error correction mechanisms. To address these challenges, we propose TrigReason, a trigger-based collaborative reasoning framework that replaces continuous polling with selective intervention. TrigReason delegates most reasoning to the SRM and activates LRM intervention only when necessary-during initial strategic planning (strategic priming trigger), upon detecting extraordinary overconfidence (cognitive offload trigger), or when reasoning falls into unproductive loops (intervention request trigger). The evaluation results on AIME24, AIME25, and GPQA-D indicate that TrigReason matches the accuracy of full LRMs and SpecReason, while offloading 1.70x - 4.79x more reasoning steps to SRMs. Under edge-cloud conditions, TrigReason reduces latency by 43.9\% and API cost by 73.3\%. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/QQQ-yi/TrigReason}{https://github.com/QQQ-yi/TrigReason}

AI.tldr: TrigReason is a trigger-based collaborative reasoning framework that uses Small Reasoning Models (SRMs) for most reasoning tasks and only activates Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) when necessary via strategic triggers, achieving comparable accuracy to full LRMs while significantly improving inference efficiency.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models achieve strong performance but suffer from high inference latency due to autoregressive reasoning. While Small Reasoning Models can accelerate inference, they face three key limitations: path divergence (inability to construct strategic initial plans), cognitive overload (failure on difficult steps), and recovery inability (lack of self-reflection and error correction).
AI.method: Proposes TrigReason framework with selective intervention using three triggers: strategic priming trigger (activates LRM for initial strategic planning), cognitive offload trigger (activates LRM when SRM shows extraordinary overconfidence on difficult steps), and intervention request trigger (activates LRM when reasoning falls into unproductive loops). Replaces continuous polling with selective delegation.
AI.result: TrigReason matches the accuracy of full LRMs and SpecReason while offloading 1.70x - 4.79x more reasoning steps to SRMs. Under edge-cloud conditions, reduces latency by 43.9% and API cost by 73.3% on evaluation datasets including AIME24, AIME25, and GPQA-D.
AI.conclusion: TrigReason effectively addresses the capability boundaries of Small Reasoning Models through trigger-based selective intervention, achieving comparable accuracy to Large Reasoning Models while significantly improving inference efficiency through strategic delegation and on-demand LRM activation.

title: Multi-User mmWave Beam and Rate Adaptation via Combinatorial Satisficing Bandits
summary: We study downlink beam and rate adaptation in a multi-user mmWave MISO system where multiple base stations (BSs), each using analog beamforming from finite codebooks, serve multiple single-antenna user equipments (UEs) with a unique beam per UE and discrete data transmission rates. BSs learn about transmission success based on ACK/NACK feedback. To encode service goals, we introduce a satisficing throughput threshold $τ_r$ and cast joint beam and rate adaptation as a combinatorial semi-bandit over beam-rate tuples. Within this framework, we propose SAT-CTS, a lightweight, threshold-aware policy that blends conservative confidence estimates with posterior sampling, steering learning toward meeting $τ_r$ rather than merely maximizing. Our main theoretical contribution provides the first finite-time regret bounds for combinatorial semi-bandits with satisficing objective: when $τ_r$ is realizable, we upper bound the cumulative satisficing regret to the target with a time-independent constant, and when $τ_r$ is non-realizable, we show that SAT-CTS incurs only a finite expected transient outside committed CTS rounds, after which its regret is governed by the sum of the regret contributions of restarted CTS rounds, yielding an $O((\log T)^2)$ standard regret bound. On the practical side, we evaluate the performance via cumulative satisficing regret to $τ_r$ alongside standard regret and fairness. Experiments with time-varying sparse multipath channels show that SAT-CTS consistently reduces satisficing regret and maintains competitive standard regret, while achieving favorable average throughput and fairness across users, indicating that feedback-efficient learning can equitably allocate beams and rates to meet QoS targets without channel state knowledge.

AI.tldr: SAT-CTS: A lightweight threshold-aware policy for joint beam and rate adaptation in multi-user mmWave MISO systems that learns from ACK/NACK feedback to meet satisficing throughput targets rather than just maximizing throughput.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient beam and rate adaptation in multi-user mmWave MISO systems where BSs use analog beamforming from finite codebooks and have limited ACK/NACK feedback, without requiring channel state information. The goal is to meet specific QoS targets (satisficing throughput thresholds) rather than just maximizing throughput.
AI.method: Formulate joint beam and rate adaptation as a combinatorial semi-bandit problem over beam-rate tuples. Propose SAT-CTS (threshold-aware policy) that combines conservative confidence estimates with posterior sampling to steer learning toward meeting the satisficing threshold τ_r rather than maximizing throughput.
AI.result: When τ_r is realizable, SAT-CTS achieves constant (time-independent) cumulative satisficing regret. When τ_r is non-realizable, it incurs only finite expected transient outside committed rounds, after which regret is O((log T)²). Experiments show reduced satisficing regret, competitive standard regret, and good fairness across users in time-varying sparse multipath channels.
AI.conclusion: SAT-CTS enables feedback-efficient learning for equitable beam and rate allocation to meet QoS targets without channel state knowledge, achieving both satisficing objectives and fairness while maintaining competitive throughput performance.

title: Benchmarks for Trajectory Safety Evaluation and Diagnosis in OpenClaw and Codex: ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX
summary: As agent systems move into increasingly diverse execution settings, trajectory-level safety evaluation and diagnosis require benchmarks that evolve with them. ATBench is a diverse and realistic agent trajectory benchmark for safety evaluation and diagnosis. This report presents ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX, two domain-customized extensions that carry ATBench into the OpenClaw and OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime settings. The key adaptation mechanism is to analyze each new setting, customize the three-dimensional Safety Taxonomy over risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm, and then use that customized taxonomy to define the benchmark specification consumed by the shared ATBench construction pipeline. This extensibility matters because agent frameworks remain relatively stable at the architectural level even as their concrete execution settings, tool ecosystems, and product capabilities evolve quickly. Concretely, ATBench-Claw targets OpenClaw-sensitive execution chains over tools, skills, sessions, and external actions, while ATBench-CodeX targets trajectories in the OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime setting over repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, and runtime policy boundaries. Our emphasis therefore falls on taxonomy customization, domain-specific risk coverage, and benchmark design under a shared ATBench generation framework.

AI.tldr: ATBench introduces customizable safety evaluation benchmarks for AI agents with domain-specific extensions (Claw and CodeX) that adapt a three-dimensional safety taxonomy to different execution settings.
AI.motivation: As agent systems evolve into diverse execution settings, there's a need for evolving benchmarks to evaluate and diagnose trajectory-level safety that can keep pace with rapidly changing tool ecosystems and product capabilities.
AI.method: Analyze each new agent execution setting, customize a three-dimensional Safety Taxonomy (risk source, failure mode, real-world harm), then use this customized taxonomy to define benchmark specifications processed through a shared ATBench construction pipeline.
AI.result: Developed ATBench-Claw for OpenClaw-sensitive execution chains involving tools, skills, sessions, and external actions, and ATBench-CodeX for OpenAI Codex/runtime settings covering repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, and runtime policies.
AI.conclusion: The ATBench framework demonstrates extensibility through taxonomy customization and domain-specific risk coverage, enabling safety evaluation benchmarks to evolve with agent systems while maintaining architectural stability across changing execution environments.

title: LongAct: Harnessing Intrinsic Activation Patterns for Long-Context Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a critical driver for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). While recent advancements have focused on reward engineering or data synthesis, few studies exploit the model's intrinsic representation characteristics to guide the training process. In this paper, we first observe the presence of high-magnitude activations within the query and key vectors when processing long contexts. Drawing inspiration from model quantization -- which establishes the criticality of such high-magnitude activations -- and the insight that long-context reasoning inherently exhibits a sparse structure, we hypothesize that these weights serve as the pivotal drivers for effective model optimization. Based on this insight, we propose LongAct, a strategy that shifts from uniform to saliency-guided sparse updates. By selectively updating only the weights associated with these significant activations, LongAct achieves an approximate 8% improvement on LongBench v2 and enhances generalization on the RULER benchmark. Furthermore, our method exhibits remarkable universality, consistently boosting performance across diverse RL algorithms such as GRPO and DAPO. Extensive ablation studies suggest that focusing on these salient features is key to unlocking long-context potential.

AI.tldr: LongAct improves RL for LLMs by selectively updating weights associated with high-magnitude activations in query/key vectors during long-context processing, achieving ~8% improvement on LongBench v2 and better generalization.
AI.motivation: While RL has been used to enhance LLM reasoning, most research focuses on reward engineering or data synthesis, but few studies exploit the model's intrinsic representation characteristics. The authors observed high-magnitude activations in query/key vectors during long-context processing and hypothesized these are crucial for effective optimization.
AI.method: LongAct shifts from uniform weight updates to saliency-guided sparse updates. Inspired by model quantization (which identifies critical high-magnitude activations) and the insight that long-context reasoning has sparse structure, the method selectively updates only weights associated with significant activations.
AI.result: Achieves approximately 8% improvement on LongBench v2 and enhanced generalization on the RULER benchmark. The method shows remarkable universality, consistently boosting performance across diverse RL algorithms (GRPO and DAPO). Ablation studies confirm focusing on salient features is key to unlocking long-context potential.
AI.conclusion: Saliency-guided sparse updates based on high-magnitude activations in query/key vectors are an effective strategy for improving RL-enhanced LLM reasoning on long contexts, demonstrating better performance and generalization than uniform update approaches.

title: The Missing Knowledge Layer in AI: A Framework for Stable Human-AI Reasoning
summary:
Large language models are increasingly integrated into decision-making in areas such as healthcare, law, finance, engineering, and government. Yet they share a critical limitation: they produce fluent outputs even when their internal reasoning has drifted. A confident answer can conceal uncertainty, speculation, or inconsistency, and small changes in phrasing can lead to different conclusions. This makes LLMs useful assistants but unreliable partners in high-stakes contexts.
  Humans exhibit a similar weakness, often mistaking fluency for reliability. When a model responds smoothly, users tend to trust it, even when both model and user are drifting together. This paper is the first in a five-paper research series on stabilising human-AI reasoning. The series proposes a two-layer approach: Parts II-IV introduce human-side mechanisms such as uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces, while Part V develops a model-side Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) that detects instability and modulates generation accordingly.
  Together, these layers form a missing operational substrate for governance by increasing signal-to-noise at the point of use. Stabilising interaction makes uncertainty and drift visible before enforcement is applied, enabling more precise capability governance. This aligns with emerging compliance expectations, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001, by making reasoning processes traceable under real conditions of use.
  The central claim is that fluency is not reliability. Without structures that stabilise both human and model reasoning, AI cannot be trusted or governed where it matters most.

AI.tldr: LLMs produce fluent but unreliable outputs in high-stakes decisions. This paper series proposes stabilization mechanisms for human-AI reasoning to make uncertainty visible and enable better governance.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in high-stakes domains (healthcare, law, finance, etc.), but they produce confident outputs even when their internal reasoning is inconsistent or drifting. The fluency of LLM outputs leads users to mistakenly trust them, creating a reliability gap in critical decision-making contexts.
AI.method: This is the first paper in a five-part series proposing a two-layer stabilization approach: 1) Human-side mechanisms (Parts II-IV): uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces; 2) Model-side mechanisms (Part V): Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) that detects instability and modulates generation.
AI.result: The proposed framework increases signal-to-noise ratio at the point of use, makes uncertainty and reasoning drift visible before enforcement, and enables more precise capability governance that aligns with regulatory requirements like the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001.
AI.conclusion: Fluency should not be mistaken for reliability. Without stabilization mechanisms for both human and model reasoning, AI cannot be trusted or properly governed in high-stakes applications. The proposed two-layer approach creates an operational substrate for effective AI governance.

title: Improving Sparse Autoencoder with Dynamic Attention
summary: Recently, sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a promising technique for interpreting activations in foundation models by disentangling features into a sparse set of concepts. However, identifying the optimal level of sparsity for each neuron remains challenging in practice: excessive sparsity can lead to poor reconstruction, whereas insufficient sparsity may harm interpretability. While existing activation functions such as ReLU and TopK provide certain sparsity guarantees, they typically require additional sparsity regularization or cherry-picked hyperparameters. We show in this paper that dynamically sparse attention mechanisms using sparsemax can bridge this trade-off, due to their ability to determine the activation numbers in a data-dependent manner. Specifically, we first explore a new class of SAEs based on the cross-attention architecture with the latent features as queries and the learnable dictionary as the key and value matrices. To encourage sparse pattern learning, we employ a sparsemax-based attention strategy that automatically infers a sparse set of elements according to the complexity of each neuron, resulting in a more flexible and general activation function. Through comprehensive evaluation and visualization, we show that our approach successfully achieves lower reconstruction loss while producing high-quality concepts, particularly in top-n classification tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a sparse autoencoder using sparsemax-based attention to dynamically determine sparsity levels for each neuron, balancing reconstruction quality and interpretability in foundation models.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoders struggle with finding optimal sparsity levels - too much sparsity hurts reconstruction, too little harms interpretability. Existing activation functions like ReLU and TopK require additional regularization or careful hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Proposes a new sparse autoencoder architecture using cross-attention with latent features as queries and learnable dictionary as key/value matrices. Employs sparsemax-based attention strategy that automatically infers sparse element sets based on each neuron's complexity.
AI.result: The approach achieves lower reconstruction loss while producing high-quality concepts, particularly excelling in top-n classification tasks through comprehensive evaluation and visualization.
AI.conclusion: Dynamically sparse attention mechanisms using sparsemax effectively bridge the reconstruction-interpretability trade-off by determining activation numbers in a data-dependent manner, offering a more flexible and general activation function for feature disentanglement.

title: Cooperate to Compete: Strategic Data Generation and Incentivization Framework for Coopetitive Cross-Silo Federated Learning
comment: Competition-aware Federated Learning, Strategic data generation approaches, A payoff-redistribution based incentive mechanism, Potential game, Social welfare
summary: In data-sensitive domains such as healthcare, cross-silo federated learning (CFL) allows organizations to collaboratively train AI models without sharing raw data. However, practical CFL deployments are inherently coopetitive, in which organizations cooperate during model training while competing in downstream markets. In such settings, training contributions, including data volume, quality, and diversity, can improve the global model yet inadvertently strengthen rivals. This dilemma is amplified by non-IID data, which leads to asymmetric learning gains and undermines sustained participation. While existing competition-aware CFL and incentive-design approaches reward organizations based on marginal training contributions, they fail to account for the costs of strengthening competitors. In this paper, we introduce CoCoGen+, a coopetition-compatible data generation and incentivization framework that jointly models non-IID data and inter-organizational competition while endogenizing GenAI-based synthetic data generation as a strategic decision. Specifically, CoCoGen+ formulates each training round as a weighted potential game, where organizations strategically decide how much synthetic data to generate by balancing learning performance gains against computational costs and competition-caused utility losses. We then provide a tractable equilibrium characterization and derive implementable generation strategies to maximize social welfare. To promote long-term collaboration, we integrate a payoff redistribution-based incentive mechanism to compensate organizations for their contributions and competition-caused utility degradation. Experiments on varying learning tasks validate the feasibility of CoCoGen+. The results show how non-IID data, competition intensity, and incentives shape organizational strategies and social welfare, while CoCoGen+ outperforms baselines in efficiency.

AI.tldr: CoCoGen+ is a novel framework that integrates synthetic data generation and incentives in coopetitive federated learning to balance organizational cooperation in training with market competition, addressing non-IID data challenges while optimizing social welfare.
AI.motivation: Cross-silo federated learning in data-sensitive domains creates a coopetition dilemma: organizations want to cooperate for better models but fear strengthening competitors. Non-IID data leads to asymmetric gains, undermining sustained participation, and existing methods don't account for competition costs.
AI.method: CoCoGen+ formulates each training round as a weighted potential game where organizations strategically decide synthetic data generation amounts, balancing learning gains against computational costs and competition utility losses. It provides equilibrium characterization, implementable generation strategies, and integrates payoff redistribution-based incentives for long-term collaboration.
AI.result: Experiments validate CoCoGen+'s feasibility, showing how non-IID data, competition intensity, and incentives shape organizational strategies and social welfare. CoCoGen+ outperforms baselines in efficiency across varying learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: CoCoGen+ successfully addresses the coopetition challenge in federated learning by integrating synthetic data generation as a strategic decision with proper incentives, enabling sustainable collaboration while accounting for competition costs and non-IID data asymmetries.

title: Calibration-Gated LLM Pseudo-Observations for Online Contextual Bandits
summary: Contextual bandit algorithms suffer from high regret during cold-start, when the learner has insufficient data to distinguish good arms from bad. We propose augmenting Disjoint LinUCB with LLM pseudo-observations: after each round, a large language model predicts counterfactual rewards for the unplayed arms, and these predictions are injected into the learner as weighted pseudo-observations. The injection weight is controlled by a calibration-gated decay schedule that tracks the LLM's prediction accuracy on played arms via an exponential moving average; high calibration error suppresses the LLM's influence, while accurate predictions receive higher weight during the critical early rounds. We evaluate on two contextual bandit environments - UCI Mushroom (2-arm, asymmetric rewards) and MIND-small (5-arm news recommendation) - and find that when equipped with a task-specific prompt, LLM pseudo-observations reduce cumulative regret by 19% on MIND relative to pure LinUCB. However, generic counterfactual prompt framing increases regret on both environments, demonstrating that prompt design is the dominant factor, more important than the choice of decay schedule or calibration gating parameters. We analyze the failure modes of calibration gating on domains with small prediction errors and provide a theoretical motivation for the bias-variance trade-off governing pseudo-observation weight.

AI.tldr: LLM-augmented contextual bandit algorithm that injects LLM predictions as pseudo-observations during cold-start, with calibration-gated weight control, reducing regret by 19% on news recommendation but showing prompt design is critical.
AI.motivation: Contextual bandit algorithms suffer from high regret during cold-start phase when there's insufficient data to distinguish good arms from bad arms, which motivates the use of LLM predictions to provide guidance during this critical early period.
AI.method: Augment Disjoint LinUCB with LLM pseudo-observations: after each round, LLM predicts counterfactual rewards for unplayed arms, inject these as weighted pseudo-observations. Use calibration-gated decay schedule to control injection weight - tracks LLM prediction accuracy on played arms via exponential moving average. High calibration error suppresses LLM influence, accurate predictions get higher weight during early rounds.
AI.result: Evaluated on UCI Mushroom (2-arm) and MIND-small (5-arm news recommendation). With task-specific prompt, LLM pseudo-observations reduce cumulative regret by 19% on MIND relative to pure LinUCB. However, generic counterfactual prompt framing increases regret on both environments, showing prompt design is dominant factor, more important than decay schedule or calibration gating parameters. Also analyzed failure modes of calibration gating on domains with small prediction errors.
AI.conclusion: LLM pseudo-observations can effectively reduce cold-start regret in contextual bandits when combined with proper prompt design, but the approach is highly sensitive to prompt framing. Calibration gating helps control LLM influence, though theoretical analysis shows bias-variance trade-off governs pseudo-observation weight selection.

title: MemoSight: Unifying Context Compression and Multi Token Prediction for Reasoning Acceleration
summary: While Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables LLMs to solve challenging reasoning problems, as KV cache grows linearly with the number of generated tokens, CoT reasoning faces scaling issues in terms of speed and memory usage. In this work, we propose MemoSight (Memory-Foresight-based reasoning), a unified framework that integrates both context compression and multi-token prediction to mitigate the efficiency issues while maintaining CoT reasoning performance. Our framework adopts the same minimalist design for both context compression and multi-token prediction via special tokens and their corresponding position layout tailored to each token type. Comprehensive experiments on four reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that MemoSight reduces the KV cache footprint by up to 66% and accelerates inference by 1.56x, while outperforming existing CoT compression methods.

AI.tldr: MemoSight: A unified framework combining context compression and multi-token prediction to address Chain-of-thought reasoning's efficiency issues by reducing KV cache footprint by up to 66% and accelerating inference by 1.56x while maintaining reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning faces significant scaling issues as KV cache grows linearly with generated tokens, leading to memory and speed bottlenecks in LLM inference.
AI.method: MemoSight integrates context compression and multi-token prediction using a minimalist design with special tokens and position layouts tailored to each token type for both techniques.
AI.result: On four reasoning benchmarks, MemoSight reduces KV cache footprint by up to 66% and accelerates inference by 1.56x while outperforming existing CoT compression methods.
AI.conclusion: MemoSight successfully addresses CoT reasoning efficiency issues through a unified framework that maintains reasoning performance while significantly improving memory usage and inference speed.

title: Blazing the trails before beating the path: Sample-efficient Monte-Carlo planning
comment: Published in Neural Information Processing Systems 2016
summary: You are a robot and you live in a Markov decision process (MDP) with a finite or an infinite number of transitions from state-action to next states. You got brains and so you plan before you act. Luckily, your roboparents equipped you with a generative model to do some Monte-Carlo planning. The world is waiting for you and you have no time to waste. You want your planning to be efficient. Sample-efficient. Indeed, you want to exploit the possible structure of the MDP by exploring only a subset of states reachable by following near-optimal policies. You want guarantees on sample complexity that depend on a measure of the quantity of near-optimal states. You want something, that is an extension of Monte-Carlo sampling (for estimating an expectation) to problems that alternate maximization (over actions) and expectation (over next states). But you do not want to StOP with exponential running time, you want something simple to implement and computationally efficient. You want it all and you want it now. You want TrailBlazer.

AI.tldr: TrailBlazer is a computationally efficient Monte-Carlo planning algorithm for MDPs that achieves sample complexity guarantees based on near-optimal states quantity, avoiding exponential runtime while exploring only relevant state subsets.
AI.motivation: Current Monte-Carlo planning methods for MDPs often have exponential running time or lack sample efficiency guarantees. The paper aims to develop an algorithm that exploits MDP structure by focusing exploration on near-optimal reachable states while being simple to implement and computationally efficient.
AI.method: TrailBlazer extends Monte-Carlo sampling to problems alternating maximization (over actions) and expectation (over next states). It performs selective exploration only of states reachable by following near-optimal policies, using a generative model for planning.
AI.result: The algorithm provides sample complexity guarantees that depend on the quantity of near-optimal states rather than full state space. It achieves computational efficiency without exponential runtime, offering an implementable solution for efficient planning in MDPs.
AI.conclusion: TrailBlazer successfully addresses the need for sample-efficient Monte-Carlo planning in MDPs by focusing on near-optimal states, providing theoretical guarantees while maintaining computational efficiency and practical implementability.

title: Toward Agentic RAG for Ukrainian
comment: This paper is a research report based on our participation in the UNLP 2026 Shared Task
summary: We present an initial investigation into Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for Ukrainian, conducted within the UNLP 2026 Shared Task on Multi-Domain Document Understanding. Our system combines two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 with BGE reranking) with a lightweight agentic layer performing query rephrasing and answer-retry loops on top of Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. Our analysis reveals that retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck: agentic retry mechanisms improve answer accuracy but the overall score remains constrained by document and page identification. We discuss practical limitations of offline agentic pipelines and outline directions for combining stronger retrieval with more advanced agentic reasoning for Ukrainian.

AI.tldr: Initial investigation of Agentic RAG for Ukrainian shows retrieval quality is the main bottleneck, not agentic reasoning.
AI.motivation: To investigate Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for the Ukrainian language within the UNLP 2026 Shared Task on Multi-Domain Document Understanding.
AI.method: Combines two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 with BGE reranking) with a lightweight agentic layer performing query rephrasing and answer-retry loops on top of Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct.
AI.result: Retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck: agentic retry mechanisms improve answer accuracy but overall scores remain constrained by document and page identification.
AI.conclusion: Practical limitations exist for offline agentic pipelines; future directions should combine stronger retrieval with more advanced agentic reasoning for Ukrainian.

title: What Is the Minimum Architecture for Prolepsis? Early Irrevocable Commitment Across Tasks in Small Transformers
comment: 24 pages, 3 figures. Under review at COLM 2026. Independent replication of the rhyme-planning finding from Lindsey et al. (2025) on open-weights models; extended to factual recall
summary: When do transformers commit to a decision, and what prevents them from correcting it? We introduce \textbf{prolepsis}: a transformer commits early, task-specific attention heads sustain the commitment, and no layer corrects it. Replicating \citeauthor{lindsey2025biology}'s (\citeyear{lindsey2025biology}) planning-site finding on open models (Gemma~2 2B, Llama~3.2 1B), we ask five questions. (Q1)~Planning is invisible to six residual-stream methods; CLTs are necessary. (Q2)~The planning-site spike replicates with identical geometry. (Q3)~Specific attention heads route the decision to the output, filling a gap flagged as invisible to attribution graphs. (Q4)~Search requires ${\leq}16$ layers; commitment requires more. (Q5)~Factual recall shows the same motif at a different network depth, with zero overlap between recurring planning heads and the factual top-10. Prolepsis is architectural: the template is shared, the routing substrates differ. All experiments run on a single consumer GPU (16\,GB VRAM).

AI.tldr: Transformers make early commitments (prolepsis) that specific attention heads sustain without correction, observed in planning and factual recall tasks across different models.
AI.motivation: To understand when transformers commit to decisions and what mechanisms prevent them from correcting those commitments, replicating previous work on planning-site finding in biological models.
AI.method: Replicated Lindsey et al.'s planning-site finding experiments on open models (Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B) using contrastive logit attribution (CLTs) instead of residual-stream methods, analyzing attention head behavior across layers.
AI.result: Found that: (1) planning is invisible to residual-stream methods; CLTs are necessary; (2) planning-site spike replicates with identical geometry; (3) specific attention heads route decisions to output; (4) search requires ≤16 layers while commitment requires more; (5) factual recall shows same pattern at different depth with no head overlap.
AI.conclusion: Prolepsis is an architectural property: transformers share commitment templates but use different routing substrates for different tasks, with specific attention heads sustaining early commitments without correction.

title: Governing Reflective Human-AI Collaboration: A Framework for Epistemic Scaffolding and Traceable Reasoning
summary:
Large language models have advanced rapidly, from pattern recognition to emerging forms of reasoning, yet they remain confined to linguistic simulation rather than grounded understanding. They can produce fluent outputs that resemble reflection, but lack temporal continuity, causal feedback, and anchoring in real-world interaction. This paper proposes a complementary approach in which reasoning is treated as a relational process distributed between human and model rather than an internal capability of either.
  Building on recent work on "System-2" learning, we relocate reflective reasoning to the interaction layer. Instead of engineering reasoning solely within models, we frame it as a cognitive protocol that can be structured, measured, and governed using existing systems. This perspective emphasizes collaborative intelligence, combining human judgment and contextual understanding with machine speed, memory, and associative capacity.
  We introduce "The Architect's Pen" as a practical method. Like an architect who thinks through drawing, the human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection. By embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into human-AI interaction, the dialogue itself becomes a reasoning loop: human abstraction -> model articulation -> human reflection.
  This reframes the question from whether the model can think to whether the human-AI system can reason. The framework enables auditable reasoning traces and supports alignment with emerging governance standards, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001. It provides a practical path toward more transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use without requiring new model architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes reframing AI reasoning as a collaborative human-AI interaction process rather than an internal model capability, introducing "The Architect's Pen" method for structured reflective reasoning through dialogue.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs lack temporal continuity, causal feedback, and real-world grounding despite advanced pattern recognition; they simulate reasoning without true understanding or continuity.
AI.method: The "Architect's Pen" method treats reasoning as a relational process distributed between human and model, embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into human-AI interaction as a structured cognitive protocol.
AI.result: The approach enables auditable reasoning traces, supports alignment with governance standards (EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001), and provides transparent, controllable AI use without requiring new model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning should be treated as a property of human-AI systems rather than internal model capability, emphasizing collaborative intelligence for more accountable and governable AI applications.

title: DLink: Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge from EEG Foundation Models
summary: EEG foundation models (FMs) achieve strong cross-subject and cross-task generalization but impose substantial computational and memory costs that hinder deployment on embedded BCI systems. Knowledge distillation is a natural solution; however, conventional methods fail for EEG FMs because task-relevant semantics are often distributed across intermediate layers, and aggressive dimensionality reduction can distort oscillatory structure via representational collapse and aliasing. To address these challenges, we propose DLink (Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge), a unified framework for transferring knowledge from large EEG FMs to compact students with three key innovations: (1) a dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; (2) an EEG MiC student with a Mimic-then-Compress pipeline, which inherits high-dimensional teacher features and then applies structured spatio-temporal compression to avoid a heavy classification head; and (3) spectral distillation that aligns teacher-student representations in the frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing and temporal jitter. Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show that DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.

AI.tldr: DLink is a knowledge distillation framework that compresses large EEG foundation models into compact versions while preserving performance, addressing representational collapse and aliasing issues unique to EEG data.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models achieve good generalization but are too computationally expensive for deployment on embedded BCI systems. Conventional knowledge distillation methods fail for EEG because task-relevant semantics are distributed across layers, and dimensionality reduction distorts oscillatory structure via representational collapse and aliasing.
AI.method: Three key innovations: (1) Dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; (2) EEG MiC student with Mimic-then-Compress pipeline that inherits high-dimensional teacher features then applies structured spatio-temporal compression; (3) Spectral distillation aligning teacher-student representations in frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing/temporal jitter.
AI.result: Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.
AI.conclusion: DLink provides an effective solution for deploying EEG foundation models on resource-constrained embedded BCI systems by addressing the unique challenges of EEG knowledge distillation while maintaining performance.

title: ADAPT: Benchmarking Commonsense Planning under Unspecified Affordance Constraints
summary: Intelligent embodied agents should not simply follow instructions, as real-world environments often involve unexpected conditions and exceptions. However, existing methods usually focus on directly executing instructions, without considering whether the target objects can actually be manipulated, meaning they fail to assess available affordances. To address this limitation, we introduce DynAfford, a benchmark that evaluates embodied agents in dynamic environments where object affordances may change over time and are not specified in the instruction. DynAfford requires agents to perceive object states, infer implicit preconditions, and adapt their actions accordingly. To enable this capability, we introduce ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning. Experiments demonstrate that incorporating ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments. We also show that a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model used as the affordance inference backend outperforms a commercial LLM (GPT-4o), highlighting the importance of task-aligned affordance grounding.

AI.tldr: DynAfford is a benchmark for evaluating embodied agents' ability to handle changing object affordances, with ADAPT module enhancing existing planners through explicit affordance reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied agents focus on directly executing instructions without considering whether target objects can actually be manipulated, failing to assess available affordances in dynamic environments where object states change over time.
AI.method: Introduce DynAfford benchmark for dynamic environments where object affordances change; develop ADAPT plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning; use domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model as affordance inference backend.
AI.result: ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments; domain-adapted vision-language model outperforms GPT-4o for affordance inference, showing importance of task-aligned affordance grounding.
AI.conclusion: Explicit affordance reasoning is crucial for robust embodied agents in dynamic environments, and task-aligned vision-language models provide better affordance grounding than general-purpose LLMs.

title: When Fairness Metrics Disagree: Evaluating the Reliability of Demographic Fairness Assessment in Machine Learning
summary: The evaluation of fairness in machine learning systems has become a central concern in high-stakes applications, including biometric recognition, healthcare decision-making, and automated risk assessment. Existing approaches typically rely on a small number of fairness metrics to assess model behaviour across group partitions, implicitly assuming that these metrics provide consistent and reliable conclusions. However, different fairness metrics capture distinct statistical properties of model performance and may therefore produce conflicting assessments when applied to the same system. In this work, we investigate the consistency of fairness evaluation by conducting a systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias in machine learning models. Using face recognition as a controlled experimental setting, we evaluate model performance across multiple group partitions under a range of commonly used fairness metrics, including error-rate disparities and performance-based measures. Our results demonstrate that fairness assessments can vary significantly depending on the choice of metrics, leading to contradictory conclusions regarding model bias. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI), a measure designed to capture the degree of inconsistency across fairness metrics. We further show that disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations. These findings highlight a critical limitation in current fairness evaluation practices and suggest that single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment.

AI.tldr: Fairness evaluations in ML can produce conflicting conclusions depending on which fairness metric is used, as different metrics capture distinct statistical properties, highlighting the insufficiency of single-metric reporting.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness evaluation approaches typically use a small number of fairness metrics, assuming they provide consistent conclusions, but different metrics may produce conflicting assessments of the same system, creating a critical limitation in current evaluation practices.
AI.method: Conducted systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias using face recognition as experimental setting, evaluated performance across multiple group partitions under various fairness metrics (error-rate disparities, performance-based measures), and introduced Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI) to quantify inconsistency across metrics.
AI.result: Fairness assessments vary significantly depending on metric choice, leading to contradictory conclusions about model bias; disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations; single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment.
AI.conclusion: Current fairness evaluation practices are critically limited by their reliance on single or few metrics, as metric inconsistency undermines reliable bias assessment, necessitating multi-metric approaches and standardized disagreement quantification for more robust fairness evaluation.

title: Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model Toward Semantic and Turn-taking Robustness in Interactive Spoken Dialogue Models
summary: Achieving seamless, human-like interaction remains a key challenge for full-duplex spoken dialogue models (SDMs). Reinforcement learning (RL) has substantially enhanced text- and vision-language models, while well-designed reward signals are crucial for the performance of RL. We consider RL a promising strategy to address the key challenge for SDMs. However, a fundamental barrier persists: prevailing automated metrics for assessing interaction quality rely on superficial proxies, such as behavioral statistics or timing-prediction accuracy, failing to provide reliable reward signals for RL. On the other hand, human evaluations, despite their richness, remain costly, inconsistent, and difficult to scale. We tackle this critical barrier by proposing a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model, which is trained to understand complex interaction dynamics using a detailed taxonomy and an annotated dataset, produces a single score and, crucially, provides separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing. Such dual outputs furnish precise diagnostic feedback for SDMs and deliver a dependable, instructive reward signal suitable for online reinforcement learning. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across a wide spectrum of datasets, spanning synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.

AI.tldr: A dual-axis generative reward model is proposed to provide reliable reward signals for reinforcement learning in spoken dialogue models by separately evaluating semantic quality and interaction timing.
AI.motivation: While reinforcement learning (RL) has enhanced other language models and could help achieve human-like interaction in spoken dialogue models, current automated metrics for interaction quality are superficial and unreliable for RL rewards, and human evaluations are too costly and inconsistent to scale.
AI.method: Proposes a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model trained on a detailed taxonomy and annotated dataset to understand complex interaction dynamics. The model produces both a single overall score and separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing.
AI.result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across various datasets, including both synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.
AI.conclusion: The dual-axis reward model successfully addresses the critical barrier in RL for spoken dialogue models by providing precise diagnostic feedback and dependable reward signals suitable for online reinforcement learning.

title: No More Guessing: a Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning
summary:
Gradient inversion attacks threaten client privacy in federated learning by reconstructing training samples from clients' shared gradients. Gradients aggregate contributions from multiple records and existing attacks may fail to disentangle them, yielding incorrect reconstructions with no intrinsic way to certify success. In vision and language, attackers may fall back on human inspection to judge reconstruction plausibility, but this is far less feasible for numerical tabular records, fueling the impression that tabular data is less vulnerable.
  We challenge this perception by proposing a verifiable gradient inversion attack (VGIA) that provides an explicit certificate of correctness for reconstructed samples. Our method adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage: the activation boundary of a fully connected layer defines a hyperplane in input space. VGIA introduces an algebraic, subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, VGIA recovers the corresponding feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via a lightweight optimization step.
  Experiments on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes demonstrate exact record and target recovery in regimes where existing state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. Compared to prior geometric approaches, VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.

AI.tldr: VGIA is a verifiable gradient inversion attack for tabular data that provides certificates of correctness for reconstructed samples, challenging the perception that tabular data is less vulnerable to gradient attacks.
AI.motivation: Current gradient inversion attacks struggle to disentangle contributions from multiple records in federated learning, lack intrinsic certification of success, and are especially problematic for numerical tabular data where human inspection is infeasible, creating a false sense of security about tabular data vulnerability.
AI.method: VGIA adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage, using hyperplane boundaries from fully connected layers to isolate individual records. It introduces an algebraic subspace-based verification test to detect when hyperplane-delimited regions contain exactly one record, then recovers feature vectors analytically and reconstructs targets via lightweight optimization.
AI.result: Experiments show exact record and target recovery on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes where state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. VGIA also allocates hyperplane queries more effectively than prior geometric approaches, enabling faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.
AI.conclusion: Tabular data is more vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks than previously assumed, and VGIA provides the first verifiable attack framework with explicit correctness certificates, enabling reliable privacy assessment in federated learning scenarios.

title: WavAlign: Enhancing Intelligence and Expressiveness in Spoken Dialogue Models via Adaptive Hybrid Post-Training
summary: End-to-end spoken dialogue models have garnered significant attention because they offer a higher potential ceiling in expressiveness and perceptual ability than cascaded systems. However, the intelligence and expressiveness of current open-source spoken dialogue models often remain below expectations. Motivated by the success of online reinforcement learning(RL) in other domains, one might attempt to directly apply preference optimization to spoken dialogue models, yet this transfer is non-trivial. We analyze these obstacles from the perspectives of reward modeling and rollout sampling, focusing on how sparse preference supervision interacts with dense speech generation under shared-parameter updates. Based on the analysis, we propose a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that makes RL practical for spoken dialogue: it constrains preference updates to the semantic channel and improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, while dynamically regulating their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients. We evaluate the method across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures, and observe consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modality-aware adaptive post-training method for spoken dialogue models using reinforcement learning to improve both semantic quality and speech expressiveness.
AI.motivation: Current open-source spoken dialogue models have limited intelligence and expressiveness compared to their theoretical potential. Direct application of preference optimization RL to spoken dialogue is challenging due to the interaction between sparse preference supervision and dense speech generation.
AI.method: A modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that constrains preference updates to semantic channels, improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, and dynamically regulates their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method makes RL practical for spoken dialogue by addressing challenges in reward modeling and rollout sampling, enabling more expressive and intelligent spoken dialogue systems.

title: Beyond the Laplacian: Doubly Stochastic Matrices for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) conventionally rely on standard Laplacian or adjacency matrices for structural message passing. In this work, we substitute the traditional Laplacian with a Doubly Stochastic graph Matrix (DSM), derived from the inverse of the modified Laplacian, to naturally encode continuous multi-hop proximity and strict local centrality. To overcome the intractable $O(n^3)$ complexity of exact matrix inversion, we first utilize a truncated Neumann series to scalably approximate the DSM, which serves as the foundation for our proposed DsmNet. Furthermore, because algebraic truncation inherently causes probability mass leakage, we introduce DsmNet-compensate. This variant features a mathematically rigorous Residual Mass Compensation mechanism that analytically re-injects the truncated tail mass into self-loops, strictly restoring row-stochasticity and structural dominance. Extensive theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that our decoupled architectures operate efficiently in $O(K|E|)$ time and effectively mitigate over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay, providing robust empirical validation on homophilic benchmarks. Finally, we establish the theoretical boundaries of the DSM on heterophilic topologies and demonstrate its versatility as a continuous structural encoding for Graph Transformers.

AI.tldr: Substituting traditional graph Laplacians with Doubly Stochastic Matrices (DSM) for GNNs, using scalable approximation and residual compensation to maintain stochasticity while encoding multi-hop proximity efficiently.
AI.motivation: Traditional GNNs rely on standard Laplacian or adjacency matrices for message passing, which may not naturally encode continuous multi-hop proximity and strict local centrality. There's a need for better structural encoding that can also mitigate over-smoothing issues.
AI.method: 1) Replace traditional Laplacian with Doubly Stochastic Matrix (DSM) derived from inverse of modified Laplacian; 2) Use truncated Neumann series to approximate DSM scalably (DsmNet); 3) Introduce Residual Mass Compensation mechanism to restore row-stochasticity by re-injecting truncated tail mass into self-loops (DsmNet-compensate).
AI.result: The proposed architectures operate efficiently in O(K|E|) time, effectively mitigate over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay, provide robust validation on homophilic benchmarks, establish theoretical boundaries on heterophilic topologies, and demonstrate versatility as structural encoding for Graph Transformers.
AI.conclusion: The DSM-based approach offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to traditional graph matrices, providing natural encoding of multi-hop proximity while maintaining efficiency and mitigating over-smoothing, with demonstrated versatility across different graph types and architectures.

title: Discovering Novel LLM Experts via Task-Capability Coevolution
summary: Frontier model developers aim to train models continually to possess emergent, diverse capabilities. To extend capabilities, the current pre-training and post-training paradigm requires manually starting training runs with static datasets or reward functions every time.
