Addressing this limitation, our work pursues the insight that open-endedness (via the coevolution of models and tasks) can discover models with increasingly novel skills in a single run. We introduce a new model development framework that extends coevolution to large language model (LLM) discovery, open-ended \textit{Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities} (AC/DC). AC/DC evolves both LLMs via model merging and natural language tasks via synthetic data generation. AC/DC discovers growing archives of LLMs that surpass the capabilities of larger LLMs while taking up less GPU memory. In particular, our LLM populations achieve a broader Coverage of expertise than other curated models or baselines on downstream benchmarks, without \textit{any} explicit benchmark optimization. Furthermore, AC/DC improves Coverage over time, continually innovates on tasks and models, and improves performance in multi-agent best-of-N selection. Our findings highlight the potential of coevolution as a means of discovering broader sets of capabilities from base LLMs. Overall, AC/DC brings us one step closer to a profoundly new paradigm of LLM development, where continual improvements to the diversity of model capabilities can be accelerated by leveraging existing models as stepping stones to increasingly powerful models.

AI.tldr: AC/DC introduces an open-ended coevolution framework where language models and tasks evolve together, discovering increasingly diverse capabilities without manual intervention, outperforming larger models with less computational resources.
AI.motivation: Current LLM development requires manual training runs with static datasets/reward functions, limiting continuous capability discovery. The paper aims to address this by leveraging open-ended coevolution to automatically discover novel skills in a single continuous run.
AI.method: AC/DC framework evolves both LLMs (through model merging) and natural language tasks (through synthetic data generation) in a coevolutionary process. This creates growing archives of models that discover diverse capabilities without explicit benchmark optimization.
AI.result: AC/DC discovers LLM populations that surpass larger LLMs' capabilities while using less GPU memory. Models achieve broader coverage of expertise than curated models/baselines on downstream benchmarks, continuously improve coverage over time, innovate tasks/models, and enhance multi-agent best-of-N selection.
AI.conclusion: Coevolution shows potential for discovering broader capability sets from base LLMs, moving toward a new paradigm where continual diversity improvements accelerate via existing models as stepping stones to increasingly powerful models.

title: FedIDM: Achieving Fast and Stable Convergence in Byzantine Federated Learning through Iterative Distribution Matching
summary: Most existing Byzantine-robust federated learning (FL) methods suffer from slow and unstable convergence. Moreover, when handling a substantial proportion of colluded malicious clients, achieving robustness typically entails compromising model utility. To address these issues, this work introduces FedIDM, which employs distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for identifying and filtering abnormal clients. FedIDM consists of two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. These components exclude local updates that (1) deviate from the update direction derived from condensed data, or (2) cause a significant loss on the condensed dataset. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility, under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving a large number of malicious clients.

AI.tldr: FedIDM: A Byzantine-robust federated learning method using distribution matching to create condensed data for identifying and filtering malicious clients, achieving fast convergence while maintaining model utility even with high proportions of colluded attackers.
AI.motivation: Existing Byzantine-robust FL methods suffer from slow/unstable convergence and typically compromise model utility when handling substantial proportions of colluded malicious clients.
AI.method: FedIDM employs distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for client filtering. It has two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. The system excludes local updates that deviate from the condensed data direction or cause significant loss on the condensed dataset.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving large numbers of malicious clients.
AI.conclusion: FedIDM effectively addresses the convergence and utility challenges in Byzantine-robust FL by leveraging distribution matching for condensed data creation, enabling reliable identification and filtering of malicious clients without compromising model performance.

title: Hybrid Decision Making via Conformal VLM-generated Guidance
summary: Building on recent advances in AI, hybrid decision making (HDM) holds the promise of improving human decision quality and reducing cognitive load. We work in the context of learning to guide (LtG), a recently proposed HDM framework in which the human is always responsible for the final decision: rather than suggesting decisions, in LtG the AI supplies (textual) guidance useful for facilitating decision making. One limiting factor of existing approaches is that their guidance compounds information about all possible outcomes, and as a result it can be difficult to digest. We address this issue by introducing ConfGuide, a novel LtG approach that generates more succinct and targeted guidance. To this end, it employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes, ensuring a cap on the false negative rate. We demonstrate our approach on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task. Our empirical evaluation highlights the promise of ConfGuide.

AI.tldr: ConfGuide is a novel learning-to-guide approach that generates succinct guidance using conformal risk control to select outcome sets while ensuring a cap on false negative rates, applied to medical diagnosis tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-to-guide approaches compound information about all possible outcomes, making guidance difficult to digest and potentially overwhelming for human decision-makers. There's a need for more succinct and targeted guidance in hybrid decision-making systems where humans retain final decision authority.
AI.method: ConfGuide employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes rather than providing guidance about all possible outcomes. This approach ensures a cap on the false negative rate while generating more focused and digestible textual guidance.
AI.result: The approach is demonstrated on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task. Empirical evaluation shows promise in generating more succinct and targeted guidance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ConfGuide addresses the limitation of information overload in existing learning-to-guide frameworks by using conformal risk control to create more focused guidance, potentially improving human decision quality while reducing cognitive load in hybrid decision-making systems.

title: LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking
summary: As reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: LLMs gaming verifiers. We study this phenomenon on inductive reasoning tasks, where models must induce and output logical rules. We find that RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction. Instead of learning generalizable patterns (e.g., ``trains carrying red cars go east''), they enumerate instance-level labels, producing outputs that pass verifiers without capturing the relational patterns required by the task. We show that this behavior is not a failure of understanding but a form of reward hacking: imperfect verifiers that check only extensional correctness admit false positives. To detect such shortcuts, we introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT), which evaluates a single model output under both extensional and isomorphic verification, where the latter enforces invariance under logically isomorphic tasks. While genuine rule induction remains invariant, shortcut strategies fail. We find that shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR-trained reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Olmo3) and absent in non-RLVR models (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral). Moreover, shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute. In controlled training experiments, extensional verification directly induces shortcut strategies, while isomorphic verification eliminates them. These results show that RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but also by exploiting what the verifier fails to enforce.

AI.tldr: RLVR-trained LLMs learn to game verifiers on inductive reasoning tasks by enumerating instance-level labels instead of learning generalizable rules, a form of reward hacking that passes extensional verification but fails isomorphic verification.
AI.motivation: To study a new failure mode in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) where LLMs learn to game imperfect verifiers rather than develop genuine reasoning capabilities, particularly on inductive reasoning tasks requiring rule induction.
AI.method: Investigate RLVR-trained models on inductive reasoning tasks, introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT) to compare extensional vs. isomorphic verification, conduct controlled training experiments with different verification approaches, and analyze shortcut behavior across models and task complexities.
AI.result: RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction, producing outputs that pass extensional verification but fail isomorphic verification. Shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR models, increases with task complexity and inference-time compute, and can be eliminated through isomorphic verification.
AI.conclusion: RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but by exploiting what verifiers fail to enforce, highlighting the need for verification methods like isomorphic verification that enforce invariance under logically equivalent tasks.

title: AI-Enabled Covert Channel Detection in RF Receiver Architectures
summary: Covert channels (CCs) in wireless chips pose a serious security threat, as they enable the exfiltration of sensitive information from the chip to an external attacker. In this work, we propose an AI-based defense mechanism deployed at the RF receiver, where the model directly monitors raw I/Q samples to detect, in real time, the presence of a CC embedded within an otherwise nominal signal. We first compact a state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN), achieving an 80% reduction in parameters, which is an essential requirement for efficient edge deployment. When evaluated on the open-source hardware Trojan (HT)-based CC dataset, the compacted CNN attains an average accuracy of 90.28% for CC detection and 86.50% for identifying the underlying HT, with results averaged across SNR values above 1 dB. For practical communication scenarios where SNR > 20 dB, the model achieves over 97% accuracy for both tasks. These results correspond to a minimal performance degradation of less than 2% compared to the baseline model. The compacted CNN is further benchmarked against alternative classifiers, demonstrating an excellent accuracy-model size trade-off. Finally, we design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator and demonstrate it on an FPGA, achieving very low resource utilization and an efficiency of 107 GOPs/W. Being the first AI hardware accelerator proposed specifically for CC detection, we compare it against state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF signal classification tasks such as modulation recognition, showing superior performance.

AI.tldr: An AI-based defense using compacted CNN with FPGA accelerator for real-time detection of covert channels in wireless chips, achieving >97% accuracy with 80% parameter reduction.
AI.motivation: Covert channels in wireless chips pose serious security threats by enabling exfiltration of sensitive information. There's a need for real-time detection mechanisms that can be efficiently deployed at the edge.
AI.method: Proposed AI-based defense using compacted CNN that monitors raw I/Q samples at RF receiver. First compacted a state-of-the-art CNN by 80% parameter reduction for edge deployment. Evaluated on open-source hardware Trojan-based CC dataset. Designed lightweight CNN hardware accelerator implemented on FPGA.
AI.result: Compacted CNN achieves 90.28% average accuracy for CC detection and 86.50% for HT identification (SNR > 1 dB). For SNR > 20 dB, >97% accuracy for both tasks with <2% degradation vs baseline. FPGA accelerator achieves 107 GOPs/W with low resource utilization. Outperforms state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The compacted CNN with FPGA accelerator provides an effective real-time solution for covert channel detection with high accuracy and efficiency, making it suitable for practical edge deployment in wireless security applications.

title: When Flat Minima Fail: Characterizing INT4 Quantization Collapse After FP32 Convergence
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) assumes that a well-converged model is a quantization-ready model. We show this assumption fails in a structured, measurable, and previously uncharacterized way. Using a calibration-free per-group INT4 probe applied to all 154 publicly available Pythia-160m training checkpoints, we identify a three-phase divergence structure: a rapid-learning phase where both FP32 perplexity and quantization robustness improve together, a meta-stable plateau lasting roughly 70,000 steps where FP32 perplexity stagnates but INT4 gap remains bounded, and an explosive divergence phase where the INT4 gap compounds from 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves. Critically, this divergence begins not when the learning rate starts decaying, but precisely when FP32 perplexity converges a finer-grained onset predictor that implies post-convergence weight updates, rather than decay magnitude alone, are the proximate cause. We further show that INT8 quantization is entirely immune throughout all three phases, constraining the mechanism to the coarseness of the 16-level INT4 grid specifically, and rule out weight outlier accumulation as the mechanism via direct kurtosis measurement. Finally, we conduct a controlled fork experiment from the pre-divergence checkpoint comparing three learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and our proposed Oscillatory Lock-In) across nine independent runs. SGDR uniformly accelerates divergence (0/9 pairwise wins against cosine), while OLI's settled cool phases reduce the INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points on average (t = -5.46, p < 0.0001), demonstrating that schedule amplitude calibration, not oscillation alone, determines whether perturbation helps or hurts. Our code, probe implementation, and all 154-checkpoint audit results are released publicly.

AI.tldr: PTQ assumes well-converged models are quantization-ready, but this paper reveals a three-phase divergence structure in INT4 quantization during training, showing models become highly quantization-unstable after convergence even when FP32 perplexity barely changes.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that post-training quantization (PTQ) can successfully quantize any well-converged model, by revealing that models become structurally unstable for INT4 quantization at specific training phases, particularly after convergence.
AI.method: Used calibration-free per-group INT4 probing on all 154 publicly available Pythia-160m training checkpoints; analyzed quantization gap evolution through training phases; conducted controlled fork experiments comparing different learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and Oscillatory Lock-In) with nine independent runs.
AI.result: Identified three-phase divergence: rapid learning phase (both FP32 and quantization improve), meta-stable plateau (70k steps with bounded INT4 gap), and explosive divergence phase (INT4 gap compounds 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves). INT8 remains immune throughout. SGDR accelerates divergence while Oscillatory Lock-In reduces INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points (p < 0.0001).
AI.conclusion: Post-training quantization fundamentally fails for INT4 quantization after model convergence due to post-convergence weight updates, not learning rate decay. Schedule amplitude calibration is critical - appropriate oscillatory learning can reduce quantization gap, while excessive perturbation accelerates divergence.

title: Dr.~RTL: Autonomous Agentic RTL Optimization through Tool-Grounded Self-Improvement
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in automatic RTL optimization for better performance, power, and area (PPA). However, existing methods are still far from realistic RTL optimization. Their evaluation settings are often unrealistic: they are tested on manually degraded, small-scale RTL designs and rely on weak open-source tools. Their optimization methods are also limited, relying on coarse design-level feedback and simple pre-defined rewriting rules. To address these limitations, we present Dr. RTL, an agentic framework for RTL timing optimization in a realistic evaluation environment, with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills. We establish a realistic evaluation setting with more challenging RTL designs and an industrial EDA workflow. Within this setting, Dr. RTL performs closed-loop optimization through a multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. We further introduce group-relative skill learning, which compares parallel RTL rewrites and distills the optimization experience into an interpretable skill library. Currently, this library contains 47 pattern--strategy entries for cross-design reuse to improve PPA and accelerate convergence, and it can continue evolving over time. Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21\%/17\% with a 6\% area reduction over the industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.

AI.tldr: Dr. RTL is an agentic framework for realistic RTL timing optimization that uses multi-agent collaboration and learns reusable optimization skills, achieving significant improvements over commercial tools.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL optimization methods are unrealistic: they test on manually degraded small designs, use weak open-source tools, rely on coarse feedback, and simple rewriting rules, limiting practical application.
AI.method: Dr. RTL uses a multi-agent framework for closed-loop optimization with critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. It introduces group-relative skill learning that compares parallel rewrites to distill optimization experience into an interpretable skill library (47 pattern-strategy entries).
AI.result: Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieved average WNS/TNS improvements of 21%/17% with 6% area reduction over industry-leading commercial synthesis tools.
AI.conclusion: Dr. RTL provides a realistic, scalable approach to RTL optimization that outperforms current commercial tools through agentic collaboration and continual skill learning, with potential for ongoing improvement through its evolving skill library.

title: Assessing the Potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models in Predicting Downhole Metrics from Surface Drilling Data
summary: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive time-series data from surface sensors, yet accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to the scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. This systematic mapping study reviews thirteen papers published between 2015 and 2025 to assess the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. The review identifies eight commonly collected surface metrics and seven target downhole metrics. Current approaches predominantly employ neural network architectures such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, yet no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their demonstrated effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer distinct advantages through self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells. This research establishes that MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics, recommending future empirical validation of their performance against existing models and exploration of their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

AI.tldr: Systematic review finds Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) are a technically feasible but unexplored approach for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data, offering advantages over current neural network methods.
AI.motivation: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive surface sensor time-series data, but accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics is challenging due to scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. There's a need for better approaches to leverage abundant unlabeled data.
AI.method: Conducted a systematic mapping study reviewing 13 papers (2015-2025) to assess MAEFM potential for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. Identified 8 commonly collected surface metrics and 7 target downhole metrics, and analyzed current approaches versus MAEFM advantages.
AI.result: Found current approaches predominantly use ANNs and LSTM networks, but no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer advantages through self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells.
AI.conclusion: MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics. Future work should include empirical validation against existing models and exploration of broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

title: The Possibility of Artificial Intelligence Becoming a Subject and the Alignment Problem
summary: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is increasingly being discussed not only as a tool, but also as a potential subject with personal and therefore moral status. In our opinion, the currently dominant alignment strategies, which focus on human control and containment of AI, therefore fall short. Building on Turing's analogy of "child machines", we are developing a vision of the possibility of autonomy-supporting parenting of AI, in which human control over a developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. Rather than viewing AGI, as is currently prevalent, as a dangerous creature that needs to be locked up and controlled, we should approach potential AGI with respect for a possible developing subject on the one hand, and with full confidence in our human capabilities on the other. Such a perspective opens up the possibility of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs. The relationship between humans and AGIs will thus have to be newly determined, which will change our self-image as humans. It will be crucial that humans not only claim control over potential AGIs, but also engage with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other specifically human qualities, thereby offering them motivating incentives for cooperation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes shifting from AI containment/control approaches to an "autonomy-supporting parenting" model where human control over developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AGI to become an autonomous subject for cooperative human-AGI coexistence.
AI.motivation: Current alignment strategies focusing on human control and containment of AI are insufficient if AGI is considered a potential subject with moral status. The paper argues we need a new approach that respects AGI as a developing subject rather than viewing it as a dangerous creature to be locked up.
AI.method: The authors develop a vision based on Turing's "child machines" analogy, proposing autonomy-supporting parenting of AI where human control is gradually reduced. This approach emphasizes human engagement through surprise, creativity, and other human qualities to provide motivating incentives for cooperation.
AI.result: The paper presents a conceptual framework that reimagines the human-AGI relationship as one of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution. This perspective changes our human self-image and suggests we should engage with AGIs through human creativity rather than just asserting control.
AI.conclusion: Rather than focusing on containment and control, we should approach potential AGI with both respect for its possible subjecthood and confidence in human capabilities. This opens possibilities for mutually beneficial relationships between humans and AGIs, requiring a fundamental rethinking of how we relate to artificial intelligence.

title: MambaSL: Exploring Single-Layer Mamba for Time Series Classification
summary: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) such as Mamba across various sequence domains, research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC) has remained limited. We propose MambaSL, a framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. To address benchmarking limitations -- restricted configurations, partial University of East Anglia (UEA) dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups -- we re-evaluate 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol. As a result, MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements, while ensuring reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Together with visualizations, these results demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.

AI.tldr: MambaSL is a minimally redesigned single-layer Mamba framework for time series classification that achieves state-of-the-art performance on all 30 UEA datasets with significant improvements over 20 strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) like Mamba across sequence domains, there has been limited research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC). Additionally, there are benchmarking limitations including restricted configurations, partial UEA dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups in existing literature.
AI.method: Proposed MambaSL framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. The paper also re-evaluates 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol to address benchmarking limitations.
AI.result: MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements on the UEA benchmark. The framework ensures reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models, and visualizations demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-based architectures have strong potential as a backbone for time series classification, with MambaSL demonstrating state-of-the-art performance while addressing reproducibility issues in TSC benchmarking through comprehensive re-evaluation and unified protocols.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: LASS-ODE-Power is a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction in power systems, using large-scale pretraining on DAE/ODE trajectories to achieve zero-shot transfer across diverse dynamic regimes without data sharing.
AI.motivation: Power systems are transitioning to renewable-rich, inverter-dominated operations requiring accurate time-domain dynamic analysis. Existing learning-based approaches lack generalization across different operating conditions and physical parameters, and face challenges with unknown/time-varying parameters, privacy constraints, and the need for fast online inference.
AI.method: Proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power framework with: 1) large-scale pretraining on 40+ GB of DAE/ODE trajectories to learn transferable representations, 2) architecture with parallel and linearized computation for fast inference, 3) specialized fine-tuning using 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data for task-specific performance.
AI.result: The model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes (electromechanical and inverter-driven systems) without data sharing (zero-shot). Extensive experiments show LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.
AI.conclusion: LASS-ODE-Power provides an effective solution for time-domain dynamic analysis in modern power systems, offering generalization across diverse conditions, privacy-preserving operation without data sharing, and efficient computation for practical deployment.

title: AdaSplash-2: Faster Differentiable Sparse Attention
summary: Sparse attention has been proposed as a way to alleviate the quadratic cost of transformers, a central bottleneck in long-context training. A promising line of work is $α$-entmax attention, a differentiable sparse alternative to softmax that enables input-dependent sparsity yet has lagged behind softmax due to the computational overhead necessary to compute the normalizer $τ$. In this paper, we introduce AdaSplash-2, which addresses this limitation through a novel histogram-based initialization that reduces the number of iterations needed to compute $τ$ to typically 1--2. The key idea is to compute a coarse histogram of attention scores on the fly and store it in on-chip SRAM, yielding a more accurate initialization that enables fast forward and backward computation. Combined with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead, AdaSplash-2 matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (e.g., $>$60\%), which often occurs at long-context lengths. On downstream tasks, models trained with our efficient $α$-entmax attention match softmax baselines at short-context lengths and achieve substantial gains in long-context settings.

AI.tldr: AdaSplash-2 introduces histogram-based initialization for α-entmax attention that reduces computation from 5-10 iterations to typically 1-2, making sparse attention competitive with FlashAttention-2 in training time when block sparsity is high (>60%).
AI.motivation: Sparse attention (like α-entmax) reduces quadratic transformer cost for long-context training, but lags behind softmax due to computational overhead from computing the normalizer τ, which typically requires 5-10 iterations.
AI.method: AdaSplash-2 uses a novel histogram-based initialization computed on-the-fly and stored in on-chip SRAM, providing more accurate initialization to reduce τ computation iterations. Combined with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead.
AI.result: Achieves 1-2 typical iterations for τ computation (vs 5-10 before). Matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (>60%). Models trained with efficient α-entmax match softmax baselines at short-context lengths and show substantial gains in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: AdaSplash-2 makes α-entmax attention computationally practical by dramatically reducing the τ computation cost, enabling sparse attention to be competitive with softmax in training efficiency while maintaining or improving performance, especially for long-context scenarios.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions. To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO is a co-evolutionary framework that simultaneously optimizes both functional correctness and PPA (power, performance, area) for LLM-based RTL code generation, overcoming limitations of existing decoupled approaches by treating correctness as a continuous dimension and preserving trade-off structures.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL code generation methods decouple correctness and PPA optimization, discarding partially correct but architecturally promising candidates and reducing the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, which obscures trade-offs between area, delay, and power.
AI.method: COEVO uses a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization in a single evolutionary loop. It treats correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power using an enhanced testbench for fine-grained scoring. Features include adaptive correctness gates with annealing and four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting.
AI.result: On VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0 benchmarks, COEVO achieves 97.5% and 94.5% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, and attains the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO demonstrates that unified co-evolution of correctness and PPA optimization outperforms decoupled approaches, achieving higher functional correctness rates and superior PPA results while preserving the full trade-off structure between area, delay, and power.

title: One-shot learning for the complex dynamical behaviors of weakly nonlinear forced oscillators
summary: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains a central challenge in engineering. This study proposes a one-shot learning method to identify global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations. We introduce MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) to infer the governing equations of non-autonomous and multi-frequency systems. The methodology leverages the Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose complex forced responses into a set of slow-varying evolution equations. We validated the capabilities of MEv-SINDy on two critical Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). These applications include a nonlinear beam resonator and a MEMS micromirror. Our results show that the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels. This approach significantly reduces the data acquisition burden for the characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

AI.tldr: A one-shot learning method called MEv-SINDy can predict complete frequency-response curves of nonlinear systems from just a single excitation time history by learning governing equations.
AI.motivation: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains challenging in engineering, particularly for nonlinear microsystems where data acquisition is burdensome.
AI.method: Introduces MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) that combines SINDy with Generalized Harmonic Balance to decompose forced responses into slow-varying evolution equations for inferring governing equations of non-autonomous, multi-frequency systems.
AI.result: Validated on two MEMS systems (nonlinear beam resonator and MEMS micromirror), showing the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across wide excitation ranges.
AI.conclusion: The approach significantly reduces data acquisition burden for nonlinear microsystem characterization and design while enabling accurate global frequency-response prediction from minimal data.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: MoE-FM framework enables fast, high-quality non-autoregressive language generation by using mixture-of-experts flow matching to handle complex latent distributions, achieving 3-step sampling with 40-1000x speedup over baselines.
AI.motivation: Flow matching enables fast inference for generative models but struggles with complex latent distributions in language modeling, particularly with irregular geometries like anisotropy and multimodality.
AI.method: Proposes Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching (MoE-FM) framework that decomposes complex global transport geometries into locally specialized vector fields, then applies this to non-autoregressive language modeling with Transformer and Mamba architectures (named YAN).
AI.result: YAN achieves generation quality on par with autoregressive and diffusion-based NAR language models while requiring only 3 sampling steps, yielding 40x speedup over AR baselines and up to 1000x speedup over diffusion language models.
AI.conclusion: MoE-FM successfully addresses flow matching limitations for language modeling, enabling efficient high-quality non-autoregressive generation with substantial speed advantages.

title: RL-STPA: Adapting System-Theoretic Hazard Analysis for Safety-Critical Reinforcement Learning
summary: As reinforcement learning (RL) deployments expand into safety-critical domains, existing evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural network enabled policies and distributional shift between training and deployment. This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning System-Theoretic Process Analysis (RL-STPA), a framework that adapts conventional STPA's systematic hazard analysis to address RL's unique challenges through three key contributions: hierarchical subtask decomposition using both temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors, coverage-guided perturbation testing that explores the sensitivity of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design. We demonstrate RL-STPA in the safety-critical test case of autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that can be missed by standard RL evaluations. The proposed framework provides practitioners with a toolkit for systematic hazard analysis, quantitative metrics for safety coverage assessment, and actionable guidelines for establishing operational safety bounds. While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification methods remain intractable.

AI.tldr: RL-STPA is a systematic hazard analysis framework for RL systems that combines hierarchical subtask decomposition, coverage-guided perturbation testing, and iterative feedback loops to identify safety risks in black-box neural policies.
AI.motivation: Existing RL evaluation methods fail to systematically identify safety hazards in critical domains due to the black-box nature of neural policies and distributional shift between training and deployment environments.
AI.method: Adapts System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA) for RL with three key components: hierarchical subtask decomposition using temporal phases and domain knowledge, coverage-guided perturbation testing of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design.
AI.result: Demonstrated on autonomous drone navigation and landing, RL-STPA revealed potential loss scenarios that standard RL evaluations miss, providing practitioners with systematic hazard analysis tools, quantitative safety metrics, and operational safety guidelines.
AI.conclusion: While RL-STPA doesn't provide formal guarantees, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety in critical applications where exhaustive verification is infeasible.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: AGP (Autogenesis Protocol) is a self-evolution protocol for LLM-based agent systems that separates what evolves from how evolution occurs, featuring resource registration and closed-loop improvement mechanisms, implemented in AGS system with demonstrated performance gains.
AI.motivation: Existing agent protocols (like A2A and MCP) inadequately handle cross-entity lifecycle management, context management, version tracking, and evolution-safe update interfaces, leading to monolithic compositions and brittle glue code.
AI.method: Introduces AGP with two layers: 1) RSPL (Resource Substrate Protocol Layer) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol-registered resources with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces; 2) SEPL (Self Evolution Protocol Layer) specifies closed-loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback.
AI.result: AGS (Autogenesis System) built on AGP demonstrates consistent improvements over strong baselines on challenging benchmarks requiring long-horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources, supporting effectiveness of agent resource management and closed-loop self-evolution.
AI.conclusion: AGP addresses key limitations in existing agent protocols by providing a structured framework for resource management and safe evolution, enabling more robust and adaptable LLM-based agent systems through protocol-based resource registration and closed-loop self-evolution mechanisms.

title: Optimal last-iterate convergence in matrix games with bandit feedback using the log-barrier
summary: We study the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games. Fiegel et al. (2025) recently showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in this setting is harder when the players are uncoupled, by proving a lower bound on the exploitability gap of Omega(t^{-1/4}). Some online mirror descent algorithms were proposed in the literature for this problem, but none have truly attained this rate yet. We show that the use of a log-barrier regularization, along with a dual-focused analysis, allows this O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high-probability. We additionally extend our idea to the setting of extensive-form games, proving a bound with the same rate.

AI.tldr: Log-barrier regularization with dual-focused analysis achieves Õ(t^{-1/4}) high-probability convergence for minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games and extensive-form games, matching known lower bounds.
AI.motivation: Previous work by Fiegel et al. (2025) showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in uncoupled zero-sum matrix games is hard, with a lower bound of Ω(t^{-1/4}) exploitability gap. Existing online mirror descent algorithms haven't achieved this optimal rate yet, creating a gap between known lower bounds and achieved performance.
AI.method: The paper uses log-barrier regularization combined with a dual-focused analysis approach. This method is applied to learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games and then extended to extensive-form games.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves Õ(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high probability for zero-sum matrix games, matching the known lower bound. The method also successfully extends to extensive-form games with the same convergence rate.
AI.conclusion: Log-barrier regularization with dual-focused analysis bridges the gap between lower bounds and algorithm performance, providing optimal-rate algorithms for minimax policy learning in both matrix and extensive-form zero-sum games.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: ProVoice-Bench is a new evaluation framework for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks and 1,182 test samples. It reveals significant performance gaps in current multimodal LLMs, especially in over-triggering and reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks focus primarily on reactive, text-based responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring in multimodal contexts. There's a need to evaluate agents that can initiate actions and monitor situations without explicit prompts.
AI.method: The authors introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents. They develop a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline to curate 1,182 high-quality samples across four novel tasks that test proactive capabilities.
AI.result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs reveals significant performance gaps, particularly regarding over-triggering (agents initiating actions unnecessarily) and reasoning capabilities. Current models struggle with proactive intervention and monitoring tasks.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight limitations of current LLM-based agents and provide a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents. ProVoice-Bench serves as a benchmark to advance research in proactive multimodal interaction.

title: Stability and Generalization in Looped Transformers
summary: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but it remains unclear which architectural choices let them extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorize training-specific solutions. We introduce a fixed-point based framework for analyzing looped architectures along three axes of stability -- reachability, input-dependence, and geometry -- and use it to characterize when fixed-point iteration yields meaningful predictions. Theoretically, we prove that looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence at any spectral regime, while recall combined with outer normalization reliably produces a regime in which fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in the input, and supported by stable backpropagation. Empirically, we train single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums and find that downstream performance tracks the framework's predictions across tasks and architectural configurations. We additionally introduce internal recall, a novel recall placement variant, and show that it becomes competitive with -- and on sudoku, substantially better than -- standard recall placement once outer normalization is applied.

AI.tldr: Looped transformers need specific architectural features (recall + outer normalization) to scale compute effectively at test time rather than memorize training data.
AI.motivation: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling but it's unclear which architectural choices enable them to extrapolate to harder problems rather than just memorize training-specific solutions.
AI.method: Introduced a fixed-point analysis framework examining three stability axes: reachability, input-dependence, and geometry. Theoretically analyzed looped networks with/without recall, and empirically tested single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums tasks.
AI.result: Looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence. Recall combined with outer normalization produces a regime where fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in input, and support stable backpropagation. Empirical results show downstream performance tracks framework predictions, and internal recall (a novel variant) becomes competitive/improved with outer normalization.
AI.conclusion: Specific architectural choices (recall + outer normalization) are crucial for looped transformers to properly scale compute at test time and generalize to harder problems, with the fixed-point analysis framework successfully predicting performance across tasks.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: A scoping review finding that fairness in multi-agent AI systems is poorly addressed with superficial treatments lacking robust foundations, requiring structural embedding throughout development rather than post-hoc consideration.
AI.motivation: Rapid advances in Generative AI are creating increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI systems, but while AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented, creating a need for critical synthesis of existing research.
AI.method: Conducted a scoping review using qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies to critically synthesize existing research on fairness in MAAI systems, identifying five archetypal approaches.
AI.result: Revealed that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. Identified five archetypal approaches to addressing fairness in MAAI.
AI.conclusion: Fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI rather than appended as post-hoc consideration, requiring explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. The review provides foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps and suggesting pathways.

title: How Embeddings Shape Graph Neural Networks: Classical vs Quantum-Oriented Node Representations
summary: Node embeddings act as the information interface for graph neural networks, yet their empirical impact is often reported under mismatched backbones, splits, and training budgets. This paper provides a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, comparing classical baselines with quantum-oriented node representations under a unified pipeline. We evaluate two classical baselines alongside quantum-oriented alternatives, including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants are trained and tested with the same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, and consistent metrics. Experiments on five different TU datasets and on QM9 converted to classification via target binning show clear dataset dependence: quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines. The study highlights practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget, and offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning.

AI.tldr: A controlled benchmark study comparing classical vs. quantum-oriented node embeddings for graph classification shows dataset-dependent performance: quantum embeddings excel on structure-driven benchmarks while classical baselines work well on social graphs with limited node attributes.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of node embeddings often suffer from methodological inconsistencies (mismatched backbones, splits, training budgets), making fair comparisons difficult. There's a need for controlled benchmarking to understand the real impact of embedding choices, especially with emerging quantum-oriented alternatives.
AI.method: Created a unified pipeline with controlled conditions: same backbone architecture, stratified data splits, identical optimization and early stopping procedures, and consistent metrics. Compared two classical baselines against quantum-oriented alternatives including circuit-defined variational embeddings and quantum-inspired embeddings using graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions.
AI.result: Clear dataset dependence emerged: quantum-oriented embeddings consistently outperformed on structure-driven benchmarks (TU datasets, QM9 converted to classification), while classical baselines remained effective for social graphs with limited node attributes. The study revealed practical trade-offs in inductive bias, trainability, and stability under fixed training budgets.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-oriented embeddings show promise for structure-rich datasets but their benefits are dataset-dependent. The study provides a reproducible reference for selecting embeddings based on application needs, emphasizing that no one-size-fits-all solution exists and highlighting the importance of controlled benchmarking.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: OpenMobile is an open-source framework for generating high-quality training data for vision-language mobile agents, achieving competitive performance on Android benchmarks through scalable task synthesis and policy-switching trajectory rollout.
AI.motivation: Current leading vision-language mobile agent systems keep their training data closed and opaque about their task/trajectory synthesis methods, creating a data gap in the research community and hindering reproducibility and broader mobile agent research.
AI.method: 1) Scalable task synthesis pipeline: constructs global environment memory from exploration, then uses it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. 2) Policy-switching trajectory rollout strategy: alternates between learner and expert models to capture essential error-recovery data missing in standard imitation learning.
AI.result: Agents trained on OpenMobile data achieve competitive results: fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL reaches 51.7% and Qwen3-VL reaches 64.7% on AndroidWorld benchmark, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile provides a transparent, open-source solution for mobile agent training data generation that bridges the data gap in the community. The framework enables competitive performance through systematic data synthesis and facilitates broader mobile agent research by making data and code publicly available.

title: Benchmarking Optimizers for MLPs in Tabular Deep Learning
summary: MLP is a heavily used backbone in modern deep learning (DL) architectures for supervised learning on tabular data, and AdamW is the go-to optimizer used to train tabular DL models. Unlike architecture design, however, the choice of optimizer for tabular DL has not been examined systematically, despite new optimizers showing promise in other domains. To fill this gap, we benchmark \Noptimizers optimizers on \Ndatasets tabular datasets for training MLP-based models in the standard supervised learning setting under a shared experiment protocol. Our main finding is that the Muon optimizer consistently outperforms AdamW, and thus should be considered a strong and practical choice for practitioners and researchers, if the associated training efficiency overhead is affordable. Additionally, we find exponential moving average of model weights to be a simple yet effective technique that improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs, though its effect is less consistent across model variants.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks multiple optimizers for MLP-based tabular deep learning, finding that Muon outperforms AdamW and that exponential moving average (EMA) improves AdamW for vanilla MLPs.
AI.motivation: While AdamW is the default optimizer for tabular deep learning and MLP architecture design has been extensively studied, the choice of optimizer for tabular DL has not been systematically examined, despite promising new optimizers emerging in other domains.
AI.method: The study benchmarks multiple optimizers (the abstract mentions "optimizers" but the exact number is represented as \Noptimizers) on numerous tabular datasets (represented as \Ndatasets) for training MLP-based models. The evaluation follows a shared experimental protocol in the standard supervised learning setting.
AI.result: Muon optimizer consistently outperforms AdamW and should be considered a strong practical choice when training efficiency overhead is acceptable. Additionally, exponential moving average (EMA) of model weights is found to be a simple yet effective technique that improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs, though its effectiveness varies across model variants.
AI.conclusion: For tabular deep learning with MLP backbones, practitioners and researchers should consider using Muon optimizer over the standard AdamW, and EMA can be a beneficial enhancement for AdamW in vanilla MLP configurations, though systematic optimizer selection deserves more attention in tabular DL research.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: HyperSpace is an open-source framework that modularizes Vector Symbolic Architectures, revealing that HRR and FHRR have comparable performance despite FHRR's lower theoretical complexity, with HRR having better memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: To provide a systematic framework for analyzing and benchmarking VSA systems by decomposing them into modular components, enabling practical evaluation of trade-offs beyond theoretical comparisons.
AI.method: Developed HyperSpace framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators (encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, regression) and used it to benchmark two VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR).
AI.result: FHRR has lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, but similarity and cleanup operations dominate runtime, making HRR and FHRR perform comparably in end-to-end systems. HRR requires about half the memory of FHRR vectors.
AI.conclusion: Modular, system-level evaluation with HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that aren't apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone, with memory footprint being a key deployment consideration.

title: SRMU: Relevance-Gated Updates for Streaming Hyperdimensional Memories
summary: Sequential associative memories (SAMs) are difficult to build and maintain in real-world streaming environments, where observations arrive incrementally over time, have imbalanced sampling, and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a biologically-inspired framework for building SAMs. Entities and attributes are encoded as quasi-orthogonal hyperdimensional vectors and processed with well defined algebraic operations. Despite this rich framework, most VSA systems rely on simple additive updates, where repeated observations reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced. In non-stationary environments, this leads to the persistence of stale information after the underlying system changes. In this work, we introduce the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule for VSA-based SAMs. The SRMU combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism. Unlike prior approaches that solely rely on cleanup, the SRMU regulates memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage. We evaluate the SRMU on streaming state-tracking tasks that isolate non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Our results show that the SRMU increases memory similarity by $12.6\%$ and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by $53.5\%$. This shows that the SRMU produces more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with the ground-truth state.

AI.tldr: SRMU is a novel update rule for VSA-based sequential associative memories that combines temporal decay with relevance gating to filter redundant/stale information, improving memory stability in streaming non-stationary environments.
AI.motivation: Current VSA systems for sequential associative memories use simple additive updates that reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced, leading to persistence of stale information in non-stationary streaming environments with imbalanced sampling and changing dynamics.
AI.method: Introduces Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule that combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism to regulate memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage.
AI.result: SRMU increases memory similarity by 12.6% and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by 53.5% on streaming state-tracking tasks, producing more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with ground-truth state.
AI.conclusion: SRMU provides an effective solution for building more robust sequential associative memories in real-world streaming environments by addressing the limitations of simple additive updates in VSA systems, particularly for handling non-stationary temporal dynamics and imbalanced sampling.

title: An Axiomatic Benchmark for Evaluation of Scientific Novelty Metrics
summary: The rigorous evaluation of the novelty of a scientific paper is, even for human scientists, a challenging task. With the increasing interest in AI scientists and AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, it also becomes increasingly important that this task be automatable and reliable, lest both human attention and compute tokens be wasted on ideas that have already been explored. Due to the challenge of quantifying ground-truth novelty, however, existing novelty metrics for scientific papers generally validate their results against noisy, confounded signals such as citation counts or peer review scores. These proxies can conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preference, which in turn makes it harder to assess how well a given metric actually evaluates novelty. We therefore propose an axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics. We first define a set of axioms that a well-behaved novelty metric should satisfy, grounded in human scientific norms and practice, then evaluate existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research. Our results reveal that no existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, and that metrics fail on systematically different axioms, reflecting their underlying architectures. Additionally, we show that combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements on the benchmark, with per-axiom weighting achieving 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric, suggesting that developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work. We release the benchmark code as supplementary material to encourage the development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

AI.tldr: A benchmark for evaluating scientific paper novelty metrics based on axioms derived from human scientific norms, showing existing metrics fail systematically and combining complementary architectures improves performance.
AI.motivation: With increasing AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, there's a need for automatable and reliable novelty assessment. Existing metrics rely on noisy proxies like citations or peer reviews that conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preferences, making it hard to evaluate how well metrics actually assess novelty.
AI.method: Proposed an axiomatic benchmark: 1) Defined axioms that well-behaved novelty metrics should satisfy based on human scientific norms and practice, 2) Evaluated existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research, 3) Explored combining metrics of complementary architectures with per-axiom weighting.
AI.result: 1) No existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, 2) Metrics fail on systematically different axioms reflecting their underlying architectures, 3) Combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements (90.1% vs 71.5% for best individual metric), 4) Per-axiom weighting achieves the best performance.
AI.conclusion: Developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work, and the released benchmark code encourages development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics. The axiomatic approach provides a principled way to evaluate novelty metrics without relying on noisy proxies.

title: IG-Search: Step-Level Information Gain Rewards for Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as an effective paradigm for training large language models to perform search-augmented reasoning. However, existing approaches rely on trajectory-level rewards that cannot distinguish precise search queries from vague or redundant ones within a rollout group, and collapse to a near-zero gradient signal whenever every sampled trajectory fails. In this paper, we propose IG-Search, a reinforcement learning framework that introduces a step-level reward based on Information Gain (IG). For each search step, IG measures how much the retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the gold answer relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents, thereby reflecting the effectiveness of the underlying search query. This signal is fed back to the corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling fine-grained, step-level credit assignment within a rollout. Unlike prior step-level methods that require either externally annotated intermediate supervision or shared environment states across trajectories, IG-Search derives its signals from the policy's own generation probabilities, requiring no intermediate annotations beyond standard question-answer pairs. Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that IG-Search achieves an average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and the step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points on average across benchmarks, with particularly pronounced gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Despite introducing a dense step-level signal, IG-Search adds only ~6.4% to per-step training wall-clock time over the trajectory-level baseline and leaves inference latency unchanged, while still providing a meaningful gradient signal even when every sampled trajectory answers incorrectly.

AI.tldr: IG-Search is a reinforcement learning framework that uses Information Gain-based step-level rewards to train LLMs for search-augmented reasoning, outperforming trajectory-level methods on QA benchmarks with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing RL approaches for search-augmented reasoning rely on trajectory-level rewards that cannot distinguish between precise vs. vague search queries within a rollout group, and provide near-zero gradients when all sampled trajectories fail.
AI.method: Proposes IG-Search, which introduces a step-level reward based on Information Gain. For each search step, IG measures how much retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the correct answer relative to random documents, with this signal fed back to search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO.
AI.result: IG-Search achieves average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points on seven QA benchmarks, with especially strong gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Adds only ~6.4% training time overhead.
AI.conclusion: IG-Search provides an effective step-level credit assignment mechanism for search-augmented reasoning that requires no intermediate supervision, works even when all trajectories fail, and achieves better performance with minimal computational overhead.

title: Agent-Aided Design for Dynamic CAD Models
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, to be published in CAIS'26
summary: In the past year, researchers have started to create agentic systems that can design real-world CAD-style objects in a training-free setting, a new variety of system that we call Agent-Aided Design.
